Title: Project Hail Mary
Author: Andy Weir
My Review:
Project Hail Mary — Book Review (3/5)

I finally finished Project Hail Mary by Andy Weir, and overall I enjoyed it quite a bit. It was a fun read, an easy read, and one that kept me turning pages. The science was fascinating, the central friendship was excellent, and the story had enough mystery and momentum to keep me invested. That said, I don't think I loved it quite as much as many readers seem to. For me, this lands somewhere around a 3/5.

One of the biggest strengths of the novel is the science. Weir has a gift for taking complicated scientific ideas and making them feel approachable, exciting, and relevant to the story. I won't pretend I followed every calculation or scientific explanation perfectly, but I followed enough to appreciate what he was doing. The book constantly presents a problem and then invites the reader to think through possible solutions alongside the characters. That's part of what makes it so engaging.

What I especially appreciated was how grounded much of the science felt. Even when the story ventured into speculative territory, it was built upon real-world principles and ideas. It felt like science fiction that respected science rather than simply using scientific words as decoration. The result was a book that made me curious and interested rather than confused or overwhelmed.

The characters are a little more of a mixed bag.

Ryland Grace and Rocky are absolutely the stars of the show. Their relationship is easily the strongest part of the novel. Watching two intelligent beings from entirely different worlds learn to communicate, cooperate, and eventually become genuine friends was wonderful. Rocky is one of the most memorable science fiction characters I've encountered in a long time. His personality shines through despite the enormous differences between him and Grace, and the friendship between the two feels earned.

Grace also grew on me over the course of the novel. Early on, I was worried that I wasn't going to connect with him. He initially felt like the standard Andy Weir protagonist: extremely smart, constantly sarcastic, and always ready with a joke. Sometimes that style works for me, and sometimes it feels a little forced. In the opening chapters, I found myself wondering if I was just getting another version of the same character Weir has written before.

Thankfully, Grace develops beyond that. As the story unfolds and his memories return, he becomes more nuanced and sympathetic. By the end, I genuinely cared about him and was invested in his choices.

The rest of the cast, however, felt fairly thin. Most of the supporting characters exist primarily to serve the plot rather than to feel like fully realized people. Stratt is probably the most memorable among them, but even she functions more as a force of nature than as a deeply developed character. I don't necessarily think this hurts the book, since so much of the story depends on Grace's isolation, but it does create a noticeable contrast between how fleshed out Grace and Rocky feel compared to almost everyone else.

I also have mixed feelings about the amnesia structure.

On one hand, it works. It creates mystery, allows the reader to discover information alongside Grace, and provides a natural way to reveal the backstory without resorting to giant exposition dumps. On the other hand, the mechanism itself sometimes felt a little clunky. There were moments where the process seemed to boil down to Grace thinking really hard and suddenly unlocking another memory. It was effective enough, but I occasionally became aware of the author's hand moving pieces into place.

My biggest criticism, though, is the ending.

The final stretch felt surprisingly rushed compared to the rest of the novel. The book spends hundreds of pages carefully building problems, solving problems, introducing new complications, and exploring the science behind everything. Then suddenly it felt like the story hit lightspeed and raced toward the finish line.

I don't dislike where the story ends. In fact, I think Grace's ultimate decision is completely fitting for his character arc. Thematically, it makes sense. Emotionally, it makes sense. I even like the image of where we leave him.

What felt lacking was the journey to get there.

Throughout the book, there were several moments where it seemed like things simply had to go wrong because the story needed another obstacle. Every time progress was made, another disaster appeared around the corner. That's not necessarily bad storytelling—conflict is essential—but eventually it started to feel like the novel was piling on problems faster than it was creating satisfying resolutions.

By the time the ending arrived, I was expecting a little more room to breathe. Instead, it felt like the book rapidly tied off its remaining threads and came to a close. I wanted more reflection. More aftermath. More exploration of the consequences of everything that had happened. The ending wasn't bad—it just felt somewhat anti-climactic given the scale of the journey.

Even with those criticisms, I had a good time reading Project Hail Mary. The science is entertaining, the pacing is generally strong, and the friendship between Grace and Rocky carries the book. There are moments of genuine wonder, clever problem-solving, and heartfelt emotion that remind me why science fiction can be such a rewarding genre.

It's not a perfect novel. Some of the character work outside the main duo is thin. The amnesia device occasionally shows its seams. The ending moves faster than I would have liked. But it remains an enjoyable adventure filled with interesting ideas and memorable moments.

Most importantly, it was fun.

And sometimes that's enough.

Rating: 3/5

A fun, science-heavy adventure with a fantastic central friendship, fascinating ideas, and an ending that left me wishing it had spent a little more time earning its conclusion. I'm looking forward to seeing how the movie adapts it.

Title: What If Love Is the Point?: Living for Jesus in a Self-Consumed World
Author: Carlos PenaVega
My Review:
I really enjoyed What If Love Is the Point? by Alexa and Carlos PenaVega.

What I appreciated most was that this wasn’t just a book about marriage or relationships—it was a book about the gospel. Throughout their story, they continually point back to Jesus and the way He has worked in their lives. It was encouraging to read about God’s grace, redemption, and faithfulness through the experiences of two people who happen to be well-known but face many of the same struggles and questions we all do.

As the title suggests, love really is the point of this book. There are some great insights on marriage, a few helpful thoughts on parenting, and practical encouragement for everyday Christian living. One section that especially stood out to me was their discussion about praying for “EGR” (Extra Grace Required) people—the people who can be difficult to love. That was both convicting and helpful.

The book is an easy, quick read, but it still gives you plenty to think about. Whether you’re married, dating, raising kids, or simply trying to follow Christ more faithfully, there’s something here worth taking away.

Overall, I found it encouraging, Christ-centered, and uplifting. I’d definitely recommend it.

Title: Science Matters: Achieving Scientific Literacy
Author: Robert M. Hazen
My Review:
I really enjoyed Science Matters. The authors do a great job taking topics that can feel intimidating and breaking them down into something understandable and interesting. As someone who is preparing to teach science, I found myself learning a lot and appreciating how the book explained concepts in a practical, approachable way.

In fact, for most of the book, I was already thinking about rereading it someday and even recommending it to some of my students. That's how much I was enjoying it.

My one disappointment came toward the end. The book shifted from explaining scientific concepts to making arguments for evolution and against creationism. Personally, I didn't care for that section. As a Christian who believes God created the world, I felt like the discussion became less about teaching science and more about challenging a viewpoint that I hold. Others may not feel the same way, but it definitely changed my experience of the final chapters.

That said, I don't want that criticism to overshadow what I liked about the book. The vast majority of Science Matters was engaging, informative, and very well written. I learned a lot from it, and I still think it does an excellent job introducing readers to many important scientific ideas. I just wish the ending had been a little more gracious toward people who hold different beliefs about origins.

Title: Across the Airless Wilds: The Lunar Rover and the Triumph of the Final Moon Landings
Author: Earl Swift
My Review:
Review: Across the Airless Wilds by Earl Swift

I picked up Across the Airless Wilds expecting a story about the Lunar Roving Vehicle—the famous “moon buggy” from Apollo. What I found was something much richer: a story about engineering, exploration, and the people who turned an audacious idea into reality.

One of the surprises of the book was realizing how little I actually knew about the rover program. I was born in 1996, and while I learned about the Moon landings in school, I don’t remember much discussion of the later Apollo missions. If you had asked me before reading this book, I probably would have pictured a single rover driving across the lunar surface. I certainly didn’t appreciate how central the rover became to Apollo 15, 16, and 17, or how dramatically it expanded what astronauts could accomplish on the Moon.

Swift does an excellent job showing that the rover was far more than a vehicle. It was a force multiplier for science. By allowing astronauts to travel much farther from their landing sites, it transformed the final Apollo missions from brief excursions into genuine geological expeditions. The rover enabled discoveries that would have been impossible on foot.

The engineering story is where the book truly shines. The rover had to be lightweight enough to fit inside the Lunar Module, durable enough to survive launch and landing, simple enough to operate in a hostile environment, and reliable enough that failure was not an option. Every chapter reveals another challenge and another ingenious solution. Time and again I found myself amazed by what these engineers accomplished with the tools and technology available in the late 1960s and early 1970s.

Yet the book is never just about machines. Its greatest strength is the people. Behind every component, test, and design revision were engineers, technicians, managers, contractors, and astronauts working under immense pressure. Swift reminds us that great achievements are rarely the result of a single brilliant individual. They are the product of teams of talented people solving thousands of problems, one at a time.

Reading the book also left me reflecting on how we remember history. Apollo 11 understandably dominates public memory, but Across the Airless Wilds highlights how much of Apollo’s scientific legacy came from the later missions. The rover helped turn the Moon from a destination into a field laboratory, and yet many of those accomplishments have faded from public awareness.

Perhaps the most interesting aspect of reading this book in 2026 is its perspective on the future. There is an understandable sense throughout the book that Apollo’s lunar exploration era had ended, perhaps permanently. From the author’s vantage point, the rover could easily be seen as the final great chapter of humanity’s adventures on the Moon.

Today, however, the story feels different.

As Artemis prepares to return humans to the lunar surface and lay the groundwork for a long-term presence there, the rover looks less like an ending and more like a beginning. The challenges faced by Apollo’s engineers—mobility, power, reliability, human exploration in a hostile environment—are still the challenges being tackled today. Future lunar vehicles, habitats, and even eventual moon bases will build upon lessons first learned by the teams who designed that remarkable folding rover.

Across the Airless Wilds is a wonderful blend of science, engineering, history, and human determination. More than anything, it left me with a renewed appreciation for what Apollo accomplished and a renewed excitement for what may come next. The rover was not merely a machine that carried astronauts across the Moon. It was an early glimpse of humanity learning how to live and work beyond Earth.

Rating: 5/5 stars.

Title: Einstein: His Life and Universe
Author: Walter Isaacson
My Review:
When most people hear Einstein’s famous equation, E=mc^2 they usually think, “That looks important,” without really knowing what it means.

One thing Walter Isaacson does so well in Einstein: His Life and Universe is help readers understand the wonder behind it. In simple terms, Einstein discovered that matter and energy are actually connected. Even the tiniest bit of matter contains an unbelievable amount of energy because the speed of light squared is such a massive number. That realization changed physics forever. It explained how stars shine, paved the way for nuclear energy, and completely reshaped humanity’s understanding of the universe. But what amazed me most while reading this biography was realizing that these discoveries didn’t come from some unreachable superhuman mind working in isolation. They came from a curious man who constantly questioned everything around him.

This was another fantastic biography by Walter Isaacson. Isaacson has a way of making brilliant people feel approachable, and he absolutely accomplishes that here with Einstein. Instead of presenting him as just a genius with wild hair writing equations on a chalkboard, Isaacson shows him as a real person — imaginative, funny, stubborn, rebellious, deeply thoughtful, and honestly pretty flawed at times too. That balance made the book incredibly engaging.

One of my favorite parts of the book was how Isaacson explained difficult scientific concepts without making them feel overwhelming. Topics like relativity, spacetime, and quantum mechanics could easily lose an average reader, but Isaacson keeps things understandable while still respecting the complexity of the ideas. I never felt talked down to, but I also never felt completely lost. That’s a hard balance to achieve, especially in a biography about arguably the greatest scientific mind in history.

What stood out to me most, though, was Einstein’s independence. He constantly challenged assumptions that everyone else simply accepted. As a child, he disliked rigid education systems. As a scientist, he questioned foundational ideas in physics. Even socially and politically, he was willing to stand apart from the crowd. Isaacson makes it clear that Einstein’s genius wasn’t just raw intelligence — it was his willingness to think differently and follow questions wherever they led. That part of the book was honestly inspiring.

I also appreciated how honest the biography was. Isaacson doesn’t turn Einstein into some perfect hero. He talks openly about Einstein’s difficult relationships, his personal shortcomings, and the ways his obsession with ideas sometimes hurt the people closest to him. In some ways, those flaws made the story even more compelling because Einstein felt human instead of mythical.

Another fascinating section covered Einstein’s relationship with quantum mechanics. Even though he helped lay the groundwork for quantum theory, he struggled with some of its implications later in life. Isaacson presents these disagreements not as dry scientific debates, but as deeper philosophical questions about reality itself. That made the science feel alive and meaningful rather than distant and technical.

Overall, I absolutely loved this book. Walter Isaacson’s research is excellent, his writing is engaging, and his understanding of Einstein is incredibly impressive. More than just teaching me about physics, this biography reminded me how powerful curiosity can be. Einstein wasn’t simply someone who knew more than everyone else — he was someone who never stopped asking questions. That spirit of wonder comes through on almost every page, and it’s probably why this book was so enjoyable to read.

Title: Songs of the Dead (The Strata Wars, #1)
Author: Brandon Sanderson

Title: The Secret Lives of Numbers: A Hidden History of Math's Unsung Trailblazers
Author: Kate Kitagawa
My Review:
I just finished The Secret Lives of Numbers by Kate Kitagawa and Timothy Revell and WHOA — this was exactly what I was looking for right now.

This book is such a fascinating mix of the history of mathematics, the development of mathematical ideas, and the stories of the people — especially women — behind those discoveries. It does an exceptional job showing that math didn’t just appear out of nowhere as a set of formulas in a textbook. These ideas were invented, argued over, explored, and shaped across centuries by real people in real cultures facing real problems.

What I loved most was how understandable the book was. It communicates complex mathematical ideas in a way that feels conversational and human instead of intimidating. The storytelling is genuinely excellent. You move from ancient number systems to groundbreaking discoveries to overlooked mathematicians, and it all flows naturally instead of feeling like a dry history lesson.

The emphasis on women in mathematics was especially compelling. So many brilliant contributors are either ignored or barely mentioned in typical math education, and this book gives them the spotlight they deserve without feeling forced or preachy. It broadens the story of mathematics into something much richer and more global.

This is the kind of book that reminds you math is not just calculation — it is a human story. Curiosity, creativity, philosophy, patterns, mystery, persistence — all of it is wrapped up in the history of numbers.

If you love science, history, space, physics, teaching, or just learning how humans slowly figured out the universe, this book is absolutely worth reading.

Title: Failure is Not an Option: Mission Control From Mercury to Apollo 13 and Beyond
Author: Gene Kranz
My Review:
Few books manage to teach history, engineering, leadership, and human courage all at once, but Failure Is Not an Option by Gene Kranz accomplishes exactly that. More than just a memoir, this book is a front-row seat to the birth of America’s space program and the intense pressure-filled world of Mission Control during the Space Race. It is easily one of the best books available for anyone who wants to understand NASA, Apollo, and the spirit of America during the 1960s and 1970s.

What makes this book so powerful is that it is not written like a distant historical summary. Kranz was there. He lived it. He was one of the legendary flight directors who guided missions from Mercury through Apollo, including the near-disaster of Apollo 13. Every chapter feels personal because it comes from someone who experienced the tension, excitement, failures, and triumphs firsthand. You are not simply reading about history — you feel immersed in it.

One of the greatest strengths of the book is how vividly it captures the atmosphere of early NASA. Kranz describes the long hours, impossible deadlines, and relentless determination that defined the engineers and controllers who worked in Mission Control. Many of them were young, inexperienced, and learning as they went, yet they carried the enormous responsibility of sending astronauts into space and bringing them safely home. The book constantly reminds the reader that space exploration was not inevitable. It was accomplished by ordinary people willing to pursue extraordinary goals.

The sections covering the Mercury and Gemini programs are fascinating because they reveal how much trial and error occurred before Apollo ever reached the Moon. The reader sees NASA grow from a fragile organization still figuring out basic procedures into the disciplined machine capable of landing men on the lunar surface. Kranz explains complex technical situations clearly enough for non-engineers to follow while still respecting the intelligence of the reader. Even someone with little scientific background can appreciate the challenges involved.

Of course, the emotional centerpiece of the book is Apollo 13. Kranz’s account of the explosion and the desperate effort to save the crew is absolutely gripping, even if you already know the outcome. The famous phrase “failure is not an option” perfectly captures the mindset of Mission Control. The book shows how teamwork, preparation, discipline, and calm leadership allowed NASA to overcome what seemed impossible. These chapters are some of the most intense and inspiring pages I have ever read.

What gives the book even more weight is its ending. Kranz reflects on the state of America’s space program after Apollo and speaks honestly about how politics became deeply intertwined with NASA’s future. During the height of the Space Race, there was a national urgency and unity that pushed innovation forward at an astonishing pace. But after the Moon landings, shifting political priorities, budget battles, and changing public interest slowed the momentum that once carried America to the lunar surface. Kranz writes with both pride and disappointment — pride in what NASA accomplished, but disappointment that the bold spirit of exploration often became dependent on political cycles and short-term thinking. His reflections make the reader wonder what could have been achieved if the same determination of the Apollo era had continued uninterrupted.

Ultimately, *Failure Is Not an Option* succeeds because it combines historical significance with genuine humanity. Gene Kranz writes with humility, honesty, and passion, making the reader care not only about the missions but also about the people behind them. Whether you are interested in NASA, engineering, American history, leadership, or simply great storytelling, this book is essential reading. It does not just explain the Space Race — it makes you feel what it was like to live through it, and it leaves you thinking about where the future of exploration may go next.

Title: Fancy Bear Goes Phishing: The Dark History of the Information Age, in Five Extraordinary Hacks
Author: Scott J. Shapiro
My Review:
Fancy Bear Goes Phishing is a fascinating and deeply philosophical exploration of hacking, cybersecurity, and the nature of the internet itself. What stood out most to me was how much the book treats hacking not just as a technical issue, but as something tied to ethics, systems, communication, and human behavior.

At the same time, that philosophical angle was probably my biggest challenge with the book. Scott Shapiro clearly knows his material and is an excellent thinker, but the writing sometimes leaned heavily into philosophical language and abstract ideas that pulled me out of the reading experience. My own learning and communication style is much more direct and visual, so some of the more esoteric discussions were harder for me to stay engaged with.

Still, I genuinely enjoyed the book. The sections on actual hacks, internet infrastructure, and the science behind computing were incredibly engaging. As someone with a computer science background who’s currently focused more on physics, chemistry, and math, it was refreshing to dive back into the world of computers and networks. The book reminded me how fascinating computer science really is and honestly made me want to sit back down at a terminal and start programming again.

The book does move into political territory at times — particularly around Donald Trump and modern cyberwarfare — though that almost feels unavoidable given the subject matter. Overall, even when I struggled with the writing style, I found the ideas compelling and the subject matter consistently interesting.

This isn’t a light or casual cybersecurity book. It’s thoughtful, intellectual, and sometimes dense, but if you’re interested in the deeper implications of hacking and the internet, it’s absolutely worth reading.

Title: Astrophysics for People in a Hurry
Author: Neil deGrasse Tyson
My Review:
This book absolutely lives up to its title. It’s a fast, sharp, and surprisingly rich introduction to astrophysics that makes huge concepts—like dark matter, cosmic background radiation, gravity, and the structure of the universe—feel accessible without dumbing them down. Tyson has a way of compressing deep scientific ideas into short chapters that still carry real weight. It stretched me just beyond my comfort zone in the best way, constantly pulling me further into questions about physics, astronomy, and how the universe actually works.

What stood out most is how much real science is packed into such a small space. This isn’t fluff—it’s dense, meaningful content delivered efficiently. Every chapter feels like a doorway into a bigger field: cosmology, particle physics, planetary science. It genuinely sparks curiosity and makes you want to keep learning.

That said, the book isn’t just scientific—it also carries a worldview. Especially toward the end, Tyson moves from explaining the universe to interpreting it, emphasizing humanity’s connection to the cosmos in ways that reflect a naturalistic perspective. For readers approaching from a biblical framework, that shift becomes noticeable.

In short: scientifically engaging, intellectually stretching, and absolutely worth the read for its clarity and scope—just read with discernment when it moves from explanation to interpretation.

Title: The Little Book of Exoplanets
Author: Joshua N. Winn
My Review:
Joshua Winn’s The Little Book of Exoplanets sits just a bit beyond my current scientific comfort zone—but in the best way possible. At times it stretched me, even went slightly over my head, yet it never lost my attention. Winn has a way of making complex ideas feel inviting rather than intimidating.

Each chapter left me thinking more deeply about planetary science, astrophysics, general physics, and even the broader world of maths. It’s the kind of book that doesn’t just inform—it sparks curiosity. I found myself wanting to keep learning long after I put it down.

Challenging, engaging, and surprisingly fun—this is a great read for anyone willing to stretch their mind just a little further into the universe.

Title: The Wrong Stuff: How the Soviet Space Program Crashed and Burned
Author: John Strausbaugh
My Review:
Review of The Wrong Stuff by John Strausbaugh

I picked up The Wrong Stuff without having read The Right Stuff, so I wasn’t coming in with a built-in comparison—just a general interest in space, flight, and how all of this actually worked (or didn’t). And honestly, that made this book a pretty engaging entry point.

Strausbaugh tells the story of the Soviet space program in a way that feels almost unbelievable at times—like a mix of ingenuity and chaos. You get stories of rushed launches, risky decisions, and missions that seem one step away from disaster. It’s the kind of book where you keep stopping and thinking, “There’s no way they actually did that… right?” And yet, they did—and somehow still managed to rack up major “firsts” in space.

The writing is probably the biggest strength. It’s quick, sarcastic, and very readable. It doesn’t feel like a textbook at all—it feels like someone telling you wild stories from history, and that makes it easy to keep turning pages.

But the more I read, the more I started to feel the limitation: it leans too hard in one direction. The Soviets come across as reckless, sloppy, and almost lucky more than anything else. And while there’s definitely truth to the danger and pressure they operated under, it starts to feel like the book is flattening the story instead of deepening it.

That’s not just my reaction either. In a review by Jeff Foust for The Space Review, he argues the book “appears to be to summarize and synthesize what others have written” and even “intended to sensationalize” the story rather than add real depth . You can read that full review here: Read the review on The Space Review￼

And I think that nails it. The book is entertaining—but it sometimes trades understanding for storytelling. You walk away with a strong impression of chaos, but maybe not a full appreciation of the real engineering, intelligence, and determination that made those achievements possible in the first place.

So overall, I’d say this: The Wrong Stuff is a fun, engaging read that got me more interested in space history—but it’s not the whole picture. It’s probably best read as a starting point, not the final word.

Title: The Wright Brothers, David McCullough, 2015, 1st ed/prtg, HC w/DJ, nice copy
Author: David McCullough
My Review:
I just finished The Wright Brothers, and it landed at a pretty interesting moment for me.

I’ve been getting more curious lately about aerospace, astronomy, and flight—just trying to understand how things actually *work* when they leave the ground (or the planet). So I picked this up thinking it might scratch that itch a little. It did, but not in the way I expected.

McCullough keeps it very narrative-driven. It’s not a technical breakdown of aerodynamics or early aircraft design—it’s much more about the people. And that ended up being the most interesting part. Wilbur and Orville aren’t portrayed as flashy inventors or once-in-a-generation prodigies. They’re methodical, patient, and honestly kind of ordinary in their day-to-day lives. What sets them apart is how seriously they took the problem of flight—and how unwilling they were to settle for bad data or assumptions.

One thing that stuck with me was how much of their success came down to questioning what everyone else accepted. They didn’t trust existing lift tables, so they built a wind tunnel and figured it out themselves. That’s not dramatic—it’s just careful, persistent work. But it’s also a good picture of what real scientific thinking looks like.

From a learning standpoint, it actually made me more aware of what I *don’t* know yet. The book doesn’t go deep into the physics, so if you’re hoping to come away understanding lift equations or airflow in detail, you won’t get that here. But it does give enough context to make you want to dig into those things on your own. It kind of sets the stage rather than explaining the whole system.

I also appreciated how grounded the story is. There’s a lot about their family, their upbringing, and their life back in Dayton. It slows the pace down at times, but it helps you see that this wasn’t some isolated “eureka” moment. It was years of incremental progress, with a lot of trial and error along the way.

If anything, the book works best as a starting point. It gives you the story behind powered flight, but it also leaves room—maybe even a need—to go learn the science behind it separately. For me, that actually lines up well with where I’m at right now. I’m not just interested in the story of flight—I want to understand the mechanics behind it, and even how it connects to bigger things like space travel.

So I’d say this is a solid, readable biography that leans heavily on story over science. It won’t teach you aerodynamics, but it might push you to go look for it next.

Title: Personal Memoirs
Author: Ulysses S. Grant
My Review:
I read The Personal Memoirs of Ulysses S. Grant after finishing Ron Chernow’s Grant last year, and I still found myself wanting more—more of Grant himself, not just the story of his life. This book absolutely delivers on that.

What surprised me most is just how real it feels. Grant doesn’t come across like a distant historical figure trying to shape how he’ll be remembered. Instead, he writes like someone sitting across the table from you, just telling you what happened. There’s a straightforwardness to his voice that I haven’t experienced in many books, especially not from someone of his stature. It almost feels conversational, even when he’s describing major battles or decisions that shaped the course of the country.

That simplicity is part of what makes the memoir so powerful. He explains military movements, strategies, and challenges in a way that’s clear without being oversimplified. You don’t need to be a Civil War expert to follow along, but you also don’t feel like anything is watered down. There’s a quiet confidence in how he writes—he doesn’t need to exaggerate anything, because the events themselves carry enough weight.

Another thing that stood out to me is his tone. Grant is remarkably humble. He gives credit to others, takes responsibility when needed, and avoids the kind of self-promotion you might expect from someone in his position. Even when he’s describing victories, there’s a sense of restraint that makes you trust him more, not less.

I will say, I was a little surprised (and maybe caught off guard) that there’s basically nothing about his presidency. At first, I wondered if something was missing, but I realized that’s really on me—I didn’t fully think through what a “memoir” is supposed to be. Grant clearly chose to focus on what mattered most to him: his military experiences and the events surrounding the war. And honestly, that focus works. It keeps the book tight and purposeful.

Reading this made me appreciate Grant even more—not just as a general, but as a communicator. His writing reflects the same qualities you’d want in a leader: clarity, steadiness, and honesty. There’s no unnecessary flourish, just a consistent effort to tell things as they happened.

If you’ve read Chernow’s biography (even if it’s been a while like it has for me) and you’re still curious about Grant, this is the perfect next step. It doesn’t just add information—it gives you Grant himself. And that’s what makes it such an excellent read.

Title: Never Panic Early: An Apollo 13 Astronaut's Journey
Author: Fred Haise
My Review:
Ooooo this one was good.

Fred Haise brings a totally different feel than the other Apollo books I’ve been reading. It’s more personal, more technical, and you really feel that airman/test pilot mindset throughout.

This book is deep—but in a fast, gripping way. It doesn’t drag. It gets right into the systems, the training, the pressure, and what it actually takes to stay calm when everything is going wrong. And honestly, that’s what makes it so hard to put down.

I think the length really works in its favor too. It’s tight. The technical language never overstays its welcome, and it keeps the pace moving the whole time.

You’re not just reading about a mission—you’re stepping into the mindset of someone trained to solve problems under pressure.

Great read. Start to finish, it held me.

Title: Apollo 8: The Thrilling Story of the First Mission to the Moon
Author: Jeffrey Kluger
My Review:
I just finished Apollo 8, and honestly… this was just awesome.

I’ve been kind of hyper-fixated on space lately, and this book hit exactly what I was looking for. It has that same feel as Apollo 13—real people, real pressure, and incredible problem-solving—but this one leans more into the wonder of it all.

What stood out to me most is how big this mission really was. These guys were the first humans to leave Earth’s orbit and go to the moon—like… actually stop and think about that. The science behind it is fascinating, but it never feels overwhelming. It actually makes it more exciting because you realize how much had to go right.

And then there’s the human side. These weren’t just astronauts—they were husbands, fathers, guys with personalities and fears. The book does a great job showing both the courage and the weight of what they were doing.

The “Earthrise” moment and the Christmas Eve broadcast were probably my favorite parts. There’s something about that scene that just hits deeper than science—it feels meaningful in a bigger way.

If you enjoy space, history, or just stories about people doing something incredibly difficult and doing it well, I’d definitely recommend this.

This one just made me love space even more.

Title: Lost Moon: The Perilous Voyage of Apollo 13
Author: Jim Lovell
My Review:
Alright—this one surprised me a little.

I went into Apollo 13 thinking, “I already know the story… something goes wrong, they fix it, everyone lives.” But the way it’s told—especially hearing it from Jim Lovell himself—makes it feel way more intense than I expected.

Once things start going wrong, it honestly feels like a slow-building crisis that just keeps getting worse. And even though you know the ending, you still catch yourself thinking, how in the world did they actually pull this off?

What I appreciated most is that it doesn’t just focus on the astronauts—it really shows how critical the people on the ground were. Mission Control comes across like a room full of people just refusing to let those guys die. It’s problem-solving at the highest level.

The audiobook version on Libby is really solid too. It’s easy to follow, not overly dramatic, but still carries the weight of what’s happening. It kind of feels like you’re listening in on history instead of just reading about it.

I will say—there are moments where it leans a little technical. Not enough to lose you, but enough where you might zone out for a second if you’re not locked in. But honestly, I think that adds to it—it reminds you this wasn’t luck, it was engineering and quick thinking.

At the end of the day, it’s not just a space story. It’s about pressure, teamwork, and staying calm when everything is going wrong.

I’d definitely recommend it—especially if you like space, problem-solving, or just a really good true story.

Title: American Lion: Andrew Jackson in the White House
Author: Jon Meacham
My Review:
Egotistical? Yes. Arrogant? Probably. But to some degree, statecraft always involves a measure of shrewdness. The character of Andrew Jackson was well suited to the demands of the White House. He was strong, intuitive, clear-eyed, and determined. In American Lion, Jon Meacham presents a vivid portrait of a president whose personality was not a liability to leadership, but rather one of its defining strengths.

Meacham’s biography focuses primarily on Jackson’s presidency, offering readers a narrative that is both engaging and deeply analytical. Rather than presenting Jackson as a distant historical figure, Meacham brings him to life—revealing a man shaped by hardship, driven by conviction, and often governed by emotion. Jackson emerges not merely as a political leader, but as a force of will whose presence reshaped the American presidency.

One of the most compelling aspects of the book is its exploration of Jackson’s leadership style. He was not a cautious or consensus-driven politician. Instead, he acted decisively, often relying on instinct and personal judgment. This is most evident in the Bank War, where Jackson dismantled the Second Bank of the United States out of a deep suspicion of concentrated financial power. Meacham portrays this not simply as policy disagreement, but as a reflection of Jackson’s broader worldview: a distrust of elites and a commitment to what he believed was the will of the common people.

At the same time, Meacham does not shy away from Jackson’s flaws. His temper, his tendency toward personal vendettas, and his controversial policies—especially regarding Native Americans—are treated with honesty and gravity. The Indian Removal Act stands as a sobering reminder that strength in leadership does not equate to moral clarity. In this way, American Lion resists the temptation to either canonize or condemn Jackson outright. Instead, it presents him as a deeply complex figure whose legacy is both influential and troubling.

Another strength of the book lies in its attention to relationships. Jackson’s loyalty to his inner circle, including figures like Martin Van Buren, highlights his relational approach to power. Yet that same loyalty often translated into hostility toward perceived enemies. Meacham captures this tension well, showing how Jackson’s personal convictions could both unify and divide.

Ultimately, American Lion is not just a biography of Andrew Jackson; it is a study of leadership itself. Meacham invites readers to consider the qualities that define effective leadership in a democratic society. Is it restraint and deliberation, or boldness and conviction? Jackson’s presidency suggests that the answer may not be simple.

In the end, Meacham leaves us with a portrait of a man who was undeniably effective, undeniably flawed, and undeniably consequential. Jackson’s story challenges us to wrestle with the reality that the very traits that enable strong leadership can also lead to profound controversy. That tension, perhaps more than anything else, is what makes American Lion such a compelling and worthwhile read.

Title: Doing Justice: A Prosecutor's Thoughts on Crime, Punishment, and the Rule of Law
Author: Preet Bharara
My Review:
Preet Bharara’s Doing Justice is a really engaging and eye-opening look at crime, punishment, and what it actually means to pursue justice. What stood out to me most is how human the whole system is. We often think of the law as something rigid and objective, but Bharara makes it clear that it depends heavily on the judgment, character, and restraint of the people enforcing it.

Coming into this book, most of my understanding of the legal system came from reading things like The Federalist Papers, Nine Black Robes, and a handful of presidential biographies. Those works helped me see the structure and philosophy behind the system, but Doing Justice brings it down to the ground level. It shows what justice looks like in real life—messy, complicated, and often filled with difficult decisions that don’t have perfect answers.

One of the most helpful parts of the book is how Bharara organizes it around a set of principles like fairness, consistency, and humility. He doesn’t just define these ideas—he shows how they actually play out in real cases. That makes the book feel practical instead of theoretical. It also highlights something I hadn’t thought much about before: sometimes justice isn’t about what you can do, but what you should do. The idea of restraint—of choosing not to act even when you have the power to—is a big theme, and it really stuck with me.

His discussion of prosecutorial (prosecutor) discretion was especially interesting. Laws might be written clearly, but applying them is anything but automatic. The decisions prosecutors make can shape lives in major ways, and those decisions require wisdom, not just knowledge. That ties back to something you see hinted at in older writings like The Federalist Papers—the system only works well when the people inside it are people of integrity.

Bharara also does a great job telling stories. He walks through different cases that show the kinds of moral and ethical challenges prosecutors face. These aren’t over-the-top or dramatic for the sake of entertainment—they’re thoughtful and help you see just how complex justice can be. Very rarely is anything completely black and white.

Another thing I appreciated is how readable the book is. Bharara doesn’t get lost in legal jargon or try to sound overly academic. He writes clearly and directly, which makes the book approachable even if you don’t have a legal background. It feels like you’re learning from someone who knows the system well but wants you to understand it too.

Overall, Doing Justice is more than just a book about law. It’s really about responsibility—what it means to make fair decisions, to hold power carefully, and to pursue justice with integrity. For me, it filled in a gap between the theory I’ve read and the reality of how things actually work. It’s a thoughtful, accessible, and genuinely enlightening read.

Title: Pet Sematary
Author: Stephen  King
My Review:
Reading Pet Sematary was a completely different experience from the other Stephen King novels I’ve worked through so far. If Salem’s Lot felt atmospheric and patient, Pet Sematary felt… eerie. Not just scary in the usual horror sense, but unsettling in a deeper, quieter way that sticks with you long after you close the book. It’s one of those stories where the dread doesn’t come from jump scares or monsters hiding in the dark. Instead, it comes from the creeping realization that something is deeply wrong, and it’s only going to get worse.

What makes the book so effective is how grounded it is in ordinary life. King spends a lot of time letting you settle into the Creed family’s world—moving to a new house, meeting neighbors, navigating family routines. It all feels incredibly normal. That normalcy is exactly what makes the supernatural elements so disturbing when they begin to surface. The pet cemetery itself starts off almost innocent, something kids made to remember their animals. But the deeper the story goes, the more that place feels like it’s humming with something ancient and wrong.

One thing that surprised me was how raw and honest the book is about adult life. There’s grief, frustration, marriage, parenting—and yes, even sexuality. The presence of sex in the story actually caught me off guard at first. It’s not something I’d really thought about encountering when picking up a horror novel. But in a strange way it makes perfect sense coming from King. His stories are so rooted in real human experience that he doesn’t shy away from the parts of life that are messy, intimate, or uncomfortable. Those elements make the characters feel more like real people than fictional archetypes, which in turn makes the horror land harder.

But what really sets Pet Sematary apart is the sense of inevitability that runs through the entire story. As a reader, you can almost see the disaster forming on the horizon long before it arrives. King lets you sit in that tension. You want the characters to make better decisions, to turn around before it’s too late, but the story keeps moving forward like gravity. It’s the kind of horror that isn’t just frightening—it’s tragic.

The book also leans heavily into themes of death, loss, and the human refusal to accept them. That’s what gives it such an eerie emotional weight. The horror isn’t just about what comes back from the grave. It’s about the desperate human desire to undo death itself—and the terrible cost of trying.

Now that I’ve read a handful of King novels—It, The Shining, Doctor Sleep, Carrie, Salem’s Lot, and now Pet Sematary—I’m starting to feel like I need to begin ranking them. The tricky part is that they’re all scary in completely different ways. It is sprawling and epic. The Shining is psychological and claustrophobic. Salem’s Lot builds dread slowly through atmosphere. But Pet Sematary might be the eeriest of the bunch. It doesn’t just scare you—it lingers with you, like a shadow you can’t quite shake.

And honestly, that lingering feeling might be the most unsettling thing King does best.

Title: ’Salem’s Lot
Author: Stephen  King
My Review:
Listening to Salem’s Lot has honestly just reminded me how ridiculously good Stephen King is at writing sentences. Not just telling a story — writing sentences. Every line feels clean and sharp and purposeful. There’s no bloat. No filler. Just this steady confidence, like he knows exactly how much detail to give you and exactly when to pull back.

What really gets me is how natural everything feels. The town of Jerusalem’s Lot doesn’t feel like a “horror setting.” It feels like a real place with real people who pay bills, argue with their spouses, and make small-town jokes. King spends time letting you sit in that normalcy. And because of that, when things start to tilt sideways, it’s deeply unsettling. He doesn’t rush the horror — he lets it creep. And somehow that restraint makes it far more powerful.

And listening to it on audiobook? Even better. You can hear the rhythm in his prose. The dialogue sounds like actual humans talking, not “characters” delivering lines. People ramble. They hesitate. They misunderstand each other. It’s subtle, but it makes everything feel grounded. Even the minor characters feel like they had lives before the book started and will continue after it ends.

What makes it even more fun for me is that I just read Dracula this year. So hearing King weave Bram Stoker into the “real world” of Salem’s Lot is such a clever move. The characters know about Dracula. They reference it. They test what they’ve learned from it. It’s this brilliant blending of fiction and reality — like King is winking at you and saying, “You’ve read the old vampire story. What if it wasn’t just a story?”

But here’s the thing — he’s not copying Stoker. He’s translating that Gothic vampire mythology into small-town America. No castles. No Transylvanian nobility. Just teachers, kids, bar owners, and quiet neighborhoods. That contrast makes the horror hit harder. Vampires aren’t invading some distant European landscape — they’re showing up on your street. In your house. At your window.

And honestly, the longer I listen, the more I just appreciate his control. He could go big and dramatic, but he often chooses subtle and human instead. The fear builds through tone shifts, silences, and tiny details. That’s confidence. That’s craft.

It’s easy to forget sometimes, because he’s so popular, that King is a genuinely exceptional prose stylist. Not just a great storyteller — a great writer. Salem’s Lot feels like proof of that. It’s literary without being pretentious, scary without being gimmicky, and deeply rooted in the real world in a way that makes the supernatural feel uncomfortably close.

And maybe that’s the real magic of it — he makes you believe, just for a second, that the old stories might still be walking around out there.

Title: Wicked: The Life and Times of the Wicked Witch of the West (The Wicked Years, #1)
Author: Gregory Maguire
My Review:
Gregory Maguire’s Wicked is one of those rare novels that reminds you why you fell in love with reading in the first place. It’s not just entertaining — it’s immersive, thoughtful, beautifully written, and layered with meaning. From the opening pages, I found myself pulled in by the prose alone. Maguire writes with a richness that makes you slow down, reread sentences, and appreciate the craft. The language has texture. It feels intentional. It feels literary in a way that most modern fantasy rarely attempts.

What surprised me most is how completely the novel stands apart from both the famous musical and the classic Oz story. It doesn’t lean on spectacle or nostalgia; instead, it builds a world that feels lived-in, political, philosophical, and real. This Oz isn’t a whimsical fairyland — it’s a civilization with prejudice, institutions, ideologies, and moral tension. Maguire doesn’t just tell a story; he constructs a history. The timeline spans years, allowing us to watch characters grow, fracture, change, and solidify. I love fantasy that covers long stretches of life because it lets you see how experiences actually shape a person. Wicked excels at that.

Elphaba herself is one of the most fascinating protagonists I’ve read in a long time. She isn’t defined by her greenness — and that’s crucial. Her appearance may be what others fixate on, but her true identity is rooted in her conscience, her convictions, and her emotional intensity. She’s morally serious, intellectually sharp, socially isolated, and painfully human. She feels less like a fictional character and more like someone history misunderstood. I found myself thinking of her as a tragic figure, not because she’s weak, but because she’s too wholehearted for the world she lives in. Her tragedy is not just suffering — it’s displacement. She’s always slightly outside the circle, never fully seen, never fully embraced.

What makes her especially compelling is that she isn’t simply a victim of circumstance. She often chooses the harder road because she refuses to betray what she believes is right. That agency elevates her from tragic casualty to tragic hero. By the end, her story doesn’t feel like the tale of a villain at all — it feels like the record of someone history flattened into a myth because myths are easier to tell than truth.

The novel’s treatment of magic reflects this same philosophy. Unlike rigid, rule-based fantasy systems, the magic in Wicked is ambiguous, symbolic, and elusive. It behaves less like a science and more like a rumor. That ambiguity perfectly reinforces the book’s central theme: truth is complicated, but stories simplify.

Ultimately, Wicked is more than a retelling. It’s a meditation on reputation, morality, and how societies create villains. It has plot strong enough to keep you turning pages, but it’s the characters — especially Elphaba — who stay with you after the book closes. This is the kind of story that lingers, the kind that makes you think, and the kind that reminds you that sometimes the most powerful magic in a novel isn’t spells at all. It’s perspective.

Title: Joseph: From Dreamer to Deliverer
Author: R.C. Sproul
My Review:
Finished reading 2026.01.13.

Fourth book read in 2026.

A great, quick read. Sproul takes a figure who often lives in the background of Genesis—at least in my own memory—and brings him sharply into focus. Joseph is familiar, but not always well-known, and this book helps bridge that gap.

As always, R.C. Sproul excels at weaving theology, history, and biblical narrative together in a way that feels both accessible and weighty. He doesn’t rush past the text or overcomplicate it. Instead, he patiently shows how God’s sovereignty, faithfulness, and providence are on display in Joseph’s life—even through suffering, betrayal, and long waiting.

It’s the kind of book that reminds you why Genesis matters and why Joseph’s story is more than just a moral lesson—it’s a testimony to a God who is always working, even when His purposes are hidden.

Highly recommended if you want a short but rich dive into Scripture.

Title: Linguistics: Why It Matters
Author: Geoffrey K. Pullum
My Review:
Finished Reading: 2026.01.11

Third book of the year!

Linguistics: Why It Matters by Geoffrey Pullum is an awesome introduction to the study of linguistics, especially for readers encountering the field for the first time. Pullum’s deep expertise is evident on every page, yet the book never feels inaccessible or overly technical.

What makes this book stand out is how clearly Pullum explains why linguistics matters at all. He moves beyond grammar rules and terminology to show how language actually works—how it’s structured, how it changes, and how misunderstandings about language shape education, culture, and public discourse. In classic Pullum fashion, he also challenges common myths about “proper” language and prescriptivism, helping readers rethink assumptions they may not even realize they hold.

The writing is engaging, thoughtful, and often quietly humorous. Pullum respects the reader’s intelligence while making complex ideas approachable through concrete examples and clear explanations. For someone new to linguistics, this book doesn’t just inform—it invites curiosity and further study.

For me, this was an excellent entry point into linguistics. It clarified what the field is about and why it’s worth taking seriously, without requiring prior background knowledge.

Highly recommended for beginners, educators, and anyone who loves language and wants to understand it better.

Title: Excavating the Evidence for Jesus: The Archaeology and History of Christ and the Gospels
Author: Titus Kennedy
My Review:
Finished 2026.01.09.

Second read of 2026.

Excavating the Evidence for Jesus by Dr. Titus M. Kennedy was an incredibly enjoyable and encouraging read. As someone who has become increasingly interested in biblical archaeology and the historical foundations of Scripture, this book felt like a natural next step. Kennedy does not write to overwhelm the reader with technical details or to win an argument, but to invite the reader into the historical world of Jesus and show how evidence—both archaeological and textual—supports the reality of His life and impact.

One of the things I appreciated most about this book is how clearly and calmly Kennedy approaches the subject. In a time when discussions about Jesus often become polarized, sensationalized, or dismissive, Kennedy takes a steady and thoughtful approach. He does not claim that archaeology can “prove” every aspect of Christianity, nor does he pretend that faith depends entirely on physical evidence. Instead, he shows how history and archaeology consistently point to Jesus as a real person who lived, taught, and was crucified in first-century Judea.

Early in the book, Kennedy addresses the idea—popular in some circles—that Jesus may not have existed at all. Rather than reacting defensively, he patiently explains why this view is rejected by nearly all professional historians. He walks through ancient non-Christian sources such as Roman and Jewish writers who mention Jesus and the early Christian movement. What stood out to me here was how restrained Kennedy is. He does not exaggerate what these sources say, but he makes it clear that they firmly establish Jesus as a historical figure. That honesty made his conclusions far more convincing.

Kennedy then moves into how archaeology fits into the conversation. He is very clear that archaeology rarely gives us direct evidence of individuals like Jesus—no personal artifacts or inscriptions with His name—but that is not how archaeology normally works. Instead, what archaeology does exceptionally well is confirm the setting of the Gospels. As Kennedy explains, the places, customs, political figures, and daily life described in the New Testament align remarkably well with what archaeologists have uncovered.

Some of the most interesting sections of the book focus on specific locations and practices mentioned in the Gospels. Sites such as the Pool of Bethesda, the Pool of Siloam, Nazareth, and Capernaum help show that the Gospel writers were deeply familiar with the geography and culture of their time. Kennedy also discusses crucifixion and burial practices, which add important historical context to the accounts of Jesus’ death. Reading these sections helped me better visualize the world of the New Testament and made the Gospel narratives feel even more grounded in real history.

Another strength of this book is how Kennedy handles the topic of the resurrection. He does not attempt to “prove” the resurrection through archaeology, and he is upfront about that. Instead, he focuses on what can be established historically: that Jesus was crucified, that His followers genuinely believed He rose from the dead, and that this belief transformed a small group of fearful disciples into a bold and growing movement. Kennedy shows that the resurrection belief appeared very early and was not a later legend. I appreciated this careful distinction between historical evidence and faith, which allowed the discussion to remain honest and credible.

Kennedy also takes time to address common claims found in documentaries, internet articles, and popular books that suggest hidden evidence, secret gospels, or pagan myths behind Christianity. Rather than mocking these ideas, he explains where they fall apart historically. This was especially helpful, as it equipped me with a clearer understanding of how scholars evaluate sources and why many popular claims simply do not hold up under serious examination.

Throughout the book, Kennedy’s tone remains respectful and inviting. He writes in a way that is easy to follow, even for readers without a background in archaeology or ancient history. At the same time, it is clear that his conclusions are grounded in serious scholarship. This balance makes the book both approachable and trustworthy.

On a personal level, Excavating the Evidence for Jesus deepened my appreciation for the historical foundations of my faith. Rather than replacing faith with evidence, the book showed how evidence can support and strengthen belief. It also sparked a desire to continue learning. Like Unearthing the Bible, this book did not close the door on the subject—it opened it wider. I found myself wanting to read more, study more, and better understand the historical world in which the Bible was written.

In conclusion, Excavating the Evidence for Jesus is a thoughtful, engaging, and encouraging book. Dr. Titus M. Kennedy does an excellent job showing that belief in Jesus is not based on myth or blind tradition, but on events rooted in real history. This book is an excellent resource for anyone curious about the historical Jesus, interested in biblical archaeology, or looking for a clearer understanding of how faith and history intersect. I would highly recommend it, especially for readers who want solid information presented in a clear and conversational way.

Title: Unearthing the Bible: 101 Archaeological Discoveries That Bring the Bible to Life
Author: Titus Kennedy
My Review:
Read 2026.01.07

First book of the year!

In Unearthing the Bible, Dr. Titus M. Kennedy offers a compelling and accessible introduction to biblical archaeology that both informs and inspires. This book succeeds not merely as a catalog of archaeological discoveries, but as a thoughtful exploration of how material evidence intersects with Scripture, history, and faith. For readers interested in the historical reliability of the Bible—and especially for those beginning to explore biblical archaeology—this work is both satisfying and motivating. Personally, this book deepened my interest in archaeology related to the biblical world and encouraged me to continue reading, researching, and studying this field more intentionally.

Dr. Kennedy writes as both a scholar and a guide. With academic credentials in archaeology and biblical studies, he demonstrates a careful commitment to evidence while maintaining respect for the biblical text. One of the book’s greatest strengths is its balanced tone. Kennedy does not treat archaeology as a tool to “prove” the Bible in a simplistic or apologetical sense, nor does he dismiss Scripture as merely theological myth. Instead, he consistently shows how archaeology functions as an independent discipline that can illuminate historical contexts, confirm certain details, challenge misunderstandings, and sharpen interpretation. This measured approach builds trust with the reader and avoids the extremes that often plague discussions about archaeology and faith.

The structure of Unearthing the Bible is clear and well organized. Kennedy walks the reader through major periods of biblical history, highlighting key archaeological discoveries relevant to both the Old and New Testaments. He discusses sites, inscriptions, artifacts, and ancient texts, explaining not only what has been found but why it matters. Importantly, he also explains the limits of archaeology—what it can and cannot do. This honesty strengthens the book’s credibility and helps readers develop realistic expectations about the relationship between archaeology and Scripture.

One particularly helpful aspect of the book is Kennedy’s attention to historical methodology. He explains how archaeologists date materials, interpret inscriptions, and evaluate competing theories. For readers without a background in archaeology, these explanations are invaluable. Rather than assuming technical knowledge, Kennedy patiently introduces key concepts and terms, making the field approachable without oversimplifying it. As a result, the reader gains not only information but also a framework for thinking critically about archaeological claims encountered elsewhere—in documentaries, articles, or popular media.

The Old Testament sections are especially engaging. Kennedy addresses well-known and often debated topics such as the patriarchs, the Exodus, the conquest of Canaan, and the monarchy of Israel. He carefully navigates areas where evidence is debated, distinguishing between what is firmly established, what is plausible, and what remains uncertain. For example, rather than overstating conclusions, he shows how inscriptions, city remains, and regional data contribute to a cumulative historical picture. This approach respects both the complexity of the evidence and the integrity of Scripture.

The New Testament material is equally strong. Kennedy explores the archaeological background of the Roman world, first-century Judea, and early Christianity. Discussions of figures such as Pontius Pilate, Herod the Great, and Caiaphas demonstrate how archaeology confirms the historical grounding of the Gospel narratives. The book reinforces the fact that the New Testament was not written in a mythological vacuum but emerged within a well-documented historical setting. For readers who value the historical credibility of the Gospels, this section is particularly encouraging.

Another major strength of Unearthing the Bible is its accessibility to both lay readers and students. Kennedy avoids academic arrogance and writes with clarity and purpose. While the book is well-researched and documented, it never feels overwhelming. This makes it suitable for a wide audience: pastors, teachers, students, and serious Bible readers alike. It would work well as a supplemental text for a Bible class, a personal study resource, or an introduction for those new to archaeology.

From a faith perspective, the book is deeply encouraging without being sensational. Kennedy does not promise that archaeology will answer every question or silence every critic. Instead, he shows how archaeological discoveries consistently align with the Bible’s historical claims and cultural details. This reinforces confidence in Scripture while also cultivating intellectual humility. Faith, in Kennedy’s presentation, is not threatened by evidence but strengthened by careful engagement with it.

Personally, Unearthing the Bible has been a turning point in my interest in biblical archaeology. While I had previously encountered archaeological references in sermons, commentaries, and documentaries, this book provided a cohesive and grounded introduction to the field. It satisfied my curiosity while also revealing how much more there is to learn. Rather than closing the subject, it opened doors—prompting me to seek further reading, research, and study. That, perhaps, is one of the highest compliments a book can receive.

In conclusion, Unearthing the Bible by Dr. Titus M. Kennedy is an excellent resource for anyone interested in the historical world of the Bible. It is thoughtful, balanced, and deeply informative, offering both knowledge and perspective. Kennedy demonstrates that archaeology, when handled responsibly, is a powerful tool for understanding Scripture more fully. This book not only enriched my understanding of the Bible’s historical context but also inspired a deeper desire to continue exploring the intersection of faith, history, and archaeology. I would highly recommend it to anyone seeking a clearer, more grounded appreciation of the Bible’s place in real history.

Title: Reading Revelation Responsibly: Uncivil Worship and Witness: Following the Lamb into the New Creation
Author: Michael J. Gorman

Title: Dracula
Author: Bram Stoker
Additional Authors: Rubén Toledo, Nina Auerbach, David J. Skal

Title: The Color Out of Space
Author: H.P. Lovecraft
My Review:
The Colour Out of Space by H.P. Lovecraft delivers a uniquely eerie atmosphere, but it might not hit home for every reader. The story centers on a mysterious force from the sky that gradually destroys a rural family’s land, health, and sanity. Lovecraft sets the stage well, with a sense of mounting dread, but the pacing may feel a bit too slow for some, and the suspense, though chilling at times, lacks a consistent intensity.

Lovecraft’s prose, while rich and descriptive, is also dense and can feel dated. Written in the 1920s, the language may be a barrier for readers more accustomed to modern styles. This can make the story feel slower than it needs to be, especially in sections where the buildup feels overly prolonged. That said, those who enjoy atmospheric horror and don’t mind the older style might appreciate the detail and slow unraveling of the plot.

The central idea of an incomprehensible, alien “colour” is intriguing, but it also creates a sense of detachment. The horror here is abstract, which can be a double-edged sword—while it allows for plenty of imagination, it also makes the terror feel distant and less visceral. Some readers might crave a more tangible threat or a clearer explanation of the menace.

For fans of cosmic horror, The Colour Out of Space will certainly satisfy, offering that signature Lovecraftian sense of existential dread. However, if you’re not already invested in Lovecraft’s mythos, the story may feel a little too vague and drawn out. The lack of a strong emotional connection to the characters also makes it harder to fully immerse in the horror.

Overall, The Colour Out of Space is worth a read for those curious about Lovecraft’s influence on horror fiction, but it may not be the most engaging entry point for newer readers. It’s a solid 3/5—a good concept with some chilling moments, but its slow pace and dated writing style may not appeal to everyone.

But, with all my reviews, it could’ve been because of the mood I was in. That has a large affect on whether I am enjoying something or not.

Title: Scribes and Scripture: The Amazing Story of How We Got the Bible
Author: John D. Meade

Title: Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire (Harry Potter, #4)
Author: J.K. Rowling

Title: Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban (Harry Potter, #3)
Author: J.K. Rowling
My Review:
Written Review
Well, another FUN Harry Potter book! This story was a fun, quick, interesting mystery! We learn some new things here and I'm excited to see what happens next in the series. This book felt like a small entry into the series.

This was a good and fun book! I love this magic system and I love how these characters interreact, I just wanted more out of this book and this story really.

It resolved quickly, the stakes seem very low, and things just kind of always happen for the best for our characters.

We also had some introductions to some characters that might actually only occur in this book, I haven't read ahead, but it was seemingly like they were here just for this entry in the series. We'll have to see what happens next.

Like I said, I really like this magic system and we got some new spells and new items added to the story and I'm interested to see where we go from here.

Time-Travel was something played with here, and that concerns me because how can that really be controlled? It could get very muddled if we start playing around with time, but I'll just have to see if this has much more impact on the story.

Snape will be Snape, Ron will be Ron, Hermione will be Hermione, Harry will be Harry, Hagrid will be Hagrid, come to think of it - every single character in these books, for every single book so far, has been fairly one-note. I hope there are some more character development in the next books.

Also, we get some more classes - I just want more out of this. How is Fantastic Beasts? More character perspectives?
