Spoiler Story Breakdown

Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban is the third book in the series. Harry for the first time confronts the darker side of his own past and the pasts of some of the people he loves and trusts.

Harry finds out his god-father, also a great friend to his father, is Sirius Black. Sirius Black, have we heard about him before? Not sure. We learn about the Azkaban Prison, and Dementors, some more magic.

I do like this book!

Title: Existential Physics: A Scientist's Guide to Life's Biggest Questions
Author: Sabine Hossenfelder
My Review:
Existential Physics by Sabine Hossenfelder
Book Review

When I picked up Existential Physics, I expected something closer to an introduction to physics—perhaps a beginner’s tour through particles, forces, and the nature of the universe. What I found instead was a dense and often provocative journey into the philosophical edges of science, where data meets speculation and certainty gives way to ambiguity. Sabine Hossenfelder doesn’t shy away from the big questions—free will, consciousness, the multiverse, the origin of the universe, and what might lie beyond it. But her tone is strikingly blunt: “we don’t really know,” or “this may change in a few decades.”

I don’t think I totally appreciate this book—for what it is, or maybe for who it’s written for. The bar of knowledge is high—unapologetically so—and while I could follow along most of the way, it took some effort. Hossenfelder writes like someone who eats quantum field theory for breakfast, and she doesn’t slow down to make sure you packed a snack. There are pages that feel like you’re being casually handed a graduate-level lecture while you’re still lacing up your shoes. If you’re not a desperate physics enthusiast—or someone who enjoys being humbled by concepts that bend time and logic—you may find yourself blinking at the page, wondering what just happened.

And when I say dense, I do mean dense. This isn’t “Physics for Poets.” This is “Physics for People Who Might Secretly Be Part of the CERN Staff.” And if you don’t get that joke, read this: CERN Super Collider. There’s beauty in that, no doubt—but also a lot of math-fog, and moments where the philosophical detours feel less like mile markers and more like sudden cliffs. It’s not that the book is inaccessible; it’s just that it doesn’t invite you in.

Still—I made it. Mostly. And I’m glad I did. But would I recommend this to the curious casual reader? Probably not. Unless that reader likes being repeatedly told “we don’t know” in increasingly complex ways.

As a Christian, I found myself regularly clashing with the worldview presented here. Hossenfelder is openly agnostic and often dismissive of religious or metaphysical explanations. She argues that physics must remain confined to what is observable and measurable, which naturally excludes questions of God, morality, and ultimate purpose. At times, her insistence on this boundary makes the book feel intellectually narrow, even while exploring some of the broadest questions imaginable. The level of scientific abstraction also occasionally creates a barrier—more philosophical than practical, and more speculative than certain.

And yet—this book did something deeply valuable for me. It made the limits of science unmistakably clear. For all its brilliance, science cannot tell us why the universe exists, only how it behaves. It cannot explain where meaning comes from, or why we hunger for beauty, purpose, and justice. The further I read, the more often I encountered answers wrapped in uncertainty: not just “we don’t know,” but “we may never know.” And even when science speaks with confidence, that confidence is almost always provisional—subject to revision by the next discovery.

That contrast brought my faith into sharper relief. Scientific theories may evolve or unravel, but the Word of God does not change. Scripture offers something science cannot: a consistent, trustworthy explanation of who we are, why we’re here, what’s gone wrong with the world, and how redemption is possible. It speaks with clarity where physics falters. It reveals a Designer, not just design; a purpose, not just pattern.

“Forever, O Lord, your word is firmly fixed in the heavens.” – Psalm 119:89

“Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever.” – Hebrews 13:8

So while I didn’t fully “appreciate” this book in the way it may have been intended, I do appreciate what it revealed. It uncovered a profound hunger—one that physics cannot satisfy. That hunger, I believe, points us not to deeper math or more data, but to the One who made it all.

Title: Fostering Resilient Learners: Strategies for Creating a Trauma-Sensitive Classroom
Author: Kristin Van Marter Souers
My Review:
Fostering Resilient Learners by Kristin Souers with Pete Hall
Book Review

As someone who regularly teaches and disciples others, though not in a traditional classroom, I found Fostering Resilient Learners to be deeply insightful and incredibly helpful. Whether you're working with youth, adults, or any community impacted by hardship and instability, this book offers a powerful lens for understanding how trauma shapes behavior, relationships, and learning.

Trauma is something I think needs to be more talked about, with care and understanding. And this book does just that. It opens the door to conversations many of us—myself included—avoid, offering language, perspective, and practices that center around empathy, safety, and human dignity. Whether you're mentoring students, counseling families, leading small groups, or simply trying to be a safe person for others, there’s something deeply valuable here.

Souers and Hall walk readers through what it means to be a trauma-informed leader: someone who listens with intention, responds with care, and helps create spaces of emotional safety and connection. Their emphasis on regulation before discipline, grace before judgment, and presence over performance really resonated with my own experience working with people carrying deep wounds.

What stood out most to me was the practical nature of their approach. They don’t offer theory without application. They give real stories, tools, and questions that have caused me to pause and reflect on my own posture as a teacher and guide. The language of “grace,” “safety,” and “empathy” runs throughout, and for that I’m thankful. In my own teaching context, I’ve seen firsthand how relational stability and compassionate consistency can open doors to discussion and real communication—which is vital for a church setting.

That said, the book also stirred something in me that I couldn’t ignore. While it speaks often of love and healing, it frames those entirely within the self. In this model, I must be the calm in the chaos. I must be the safe harbor. I must be the emotional anchor. And though I understand the value of personal responsibility, I also felt the weight of that burden.

There’s a quiet but consistent thread woven throughout the book: the belief that we, as educators, mentors, or leaders, already possess everything necessary within ourselves to mend what’s broken in others. That if we can just remain calm enough, empathetic enough, and self-aware enough, we can be the healing presence people need. But as a Christian, I don’t believe that’s true—and I’m convinced it’s a dangerous weight to carry. Yes, we are called to love fiercely, to serve sacrificially, and to show up with compassion. But we are not the source of hope. We are not the healers. We are not enough on our own.

What was missing in this otherwise thoughtful and practical book was any recognition of a truth beyond self—a foundation stronger than human willpower or emotional regulation. Terms like grace, hope, healing, and restoration appeared often, but they were unmoored from any eternal reality, floating in a framework of self-reliance. In a world shattered by generational trauma, injustice, and deep soul wounds, that kind of foundation simply cannot hold.

We don’t just need better tools—we need transforming truth. We don’t just need inner strength—we need divine strength. And we don’t just need to foster resilient learners—we need to point them, and ourselves, to the true Rescuer. Otherwise, we risk bearing burdens we were never meant to carry.

Still, I’m grateful for this book. It challenged and equipped me. It reminded me how important it is to slow down, to see people, to create spaces where others feel safe and seen. But it also reminded me why I need Jesus—because if I try to be the Savior in the story, I’ll burn out fast. The book gives us a strong model of compassionate leadership. But as I take what I’ve learned and apply it, I know I have to anchor it in something more eternal.

Title: Strange New World: How Thinkers and Activists Redefined Identity and Sparked the Sexual Revolution
Author: Carl R. Trueman
My Review:
Strange New World by Carl R. Trueman
Subtitle: How Thinkers and Activists Redefined Identity and Sparked the Sexual Revolution


Review
I picked up Strange New World hoping for a biblically rooted response to the sexual revolution—something that would engage with the cultural moment through the lens of Scripture. While Carl Trueman does offer a helpful, and at times necessary, primer on how Western thought drifted into today’s expressive individualism, this book didn’t deliver what I had hoped for. This book is heavy on philosophy and not theology.

This work is a shortened and “more accessible” version of Trueman’s much longer and more scholarly and dense book, The Rise and Triumph of the Modern Self. And it definitely feels that way: condensed, concise, and… surface level. I think it was too condensed, honestly, I wanted more.

Table of Contents
- Welcome to This Strange New World
- Romantic Roots
- Prometheus Unbound
- Sexualizing Psychology, Politicizing Sex
- The Revolt of the Masses
- Plastic People, Liquid World
- The Sexual Revolution of the LGBTQ+
- Life, Liberty, and the Pursuit of Happiness
- Strangers in This Strange New World

Content
Trueman follows the intellectual journey from Jean-Jacques Rousseau to Sigmund Freud, tracing how philosophical and psychological shifts gradually redefined identity, morality, and sexuality—culminating in today’s sexual revolution. Rousseau laid the groundwork with his idea that true identity is found within the self, not shaped by societal expectations or traditions. The beginning of the end, in my opinion.

That turn was picked up by Romantic thinkers who celebrated personal expression as the highest virtue, “my truth”. Friedrich Nietzsche and Karl Marx then deconstructed morality and religion, paving the way for a world where God is no longer the source of truth or authority.

Finally, Freud sexualized the self, teaching that human identity is fundamentally rooted in sexual desire—and that repression of those desires leads to psychological harm. These ideas, once confined to academic circles, eventually filtered down into pop-culture through activists, institutions, and media.

If you’ve never studied the history or philosophy behind modern identity politics, Strange New World may serve as a strong on-ramp, it certainly was the starting place for me. Trueman seems to do well at connecting dots across centuries and thinkers, showing how today’s debates didn’t arise overnight—they are the result of a long and deliberate re-imagining of the self.

But if you're looking for a theological or exegetical critique of gender and sexuality ideologies—this book isn't it. There are very few direct biblical responses or sustained scriptural engagements. And to be fair, Trueman doesn’t necessarily promise them, and I should’ve paid more attention to that.

Author’s Intent
His goal is to diagnose the cultural moment and in that sense, Strange New World functions more as a Christian sociology rather than an apologetic or theological response. Something that I was actually wanting out of this book.

In this, my reaction seems to line up with many other readers and reviewers. Strange New World is widely appreciated for what it is—a clear and concise overview of how we got here culturally—but it’s also commonly noted that it lacks depth, particularly in offering a robust biblical response. It diagnoses the problem well but doesn’t walk you through the remedy. It’s a solid summary, not a full treatment. Of course, we have to remember this is a concise work based on a much larger work — I wonder if that work, The Rise and Triumph of the Modern Self, does anymore towards the biblical response.

That said, Trueman’s writing is clear, calm, and level-headed. He exposes the inner logic of our culture, and I certainly appreciate that. Still, for readers like me who are deeply invested in thinking biblically about these shifts—not just culturally or historically—it may feel incomplete. I walked away understanding better how we got here, but not much more equipped to speak the truth in love to those immersed in this worldview.

Final Thoughts
Strange New World is a valuable summary of ideas—but not a full-bodied Christian response. It shows us the world’s logic, but not God’s. For that, we’ll need to keep turning the pages—of Scripture first and foremost, and perhaps beyond this book as well.

Title: The Sunlit Man
Author: Brandon Sanderson
My Review:
Intro



Brandon Sanderson’s The Sunlit Man is awesome! There’s something special about a Cosmere book that trusts you to be paying attention. Set on a strange planet where light burns like judgment and gravity is a suggestion, the book wastes no time throwing you into chaos: chase scenes in the sky, civilizations clinging to crumbling platforms, a mysterious “torment” eating away at the main character. And behind it all, the looming shadow of forces we’ve only glimpsed in hidden epigraphs and brief interludes.



Plot



From the jump, you know you’re not getting a clean, cheery hero’s journey. Nomad is distant, guarded, and so obviously hurting that it hurts to read him. The mystery of who he is and why he runs carries the story forward like a relentless current. And Sanderson, master of structure, waits just long enough before cracking the facade wide open.



This isn’t just another Cosmere story. It’s a novel about grief, shame, and the unbearable weight of running from yourself. The protagonist, known only as Nomad, drops into a strange planet where sunlight is lethal, people leap between floating continents, and nothing is safe for long—including hope.



There’s action—plenty of it. Wild tech-magic systems, impossible environments, chase scenes that defy physics. But that’s not why this book matters. What makes The Sunlit Man unforgettable is that it’s about a man who believes he's beyond redemption. Who thinks he’s burned every bridge that ever led to peace. And what happens when, against all reason, he decides to stop running.



Thematically, this book stands tall alongside Sanderson’s best. It asks: What do you do when you can no longer believe in yourself? What does it cost to choose to stay? And can a broken person still make a difference—especially when they’re the last one who thinks they can?



This is a book that might not immediately reveal how powerful it is. It builds. It waits. It hurts. But by the final chapters, it doesn't just shine—it burns.



But here’s the brilliance: Sanderson keeps the story intimate. No Shards looming overhead. No Shardblades drawn. Just one person, on the run, trying to decide whether they’re still worth anything at all.



Secret Projects



If Tress was playful and Secret Project 3 was experimental, The Sunlit Man is raw. It feels like Cosmere Phase Two finally growing teeth—wider in scope, deeper in grief, and more connected than ever. It's the book where background characters become legends, where hidden lore steps into the sun, and where long-time fans finally feel the weight of being a worldhopper.



Prerequisites



And yet, if you’ve never read another Cosmere book? It still works. Sanderson crafts this as a fully self-contained narrative—a broken man in a brutal world, hunted for the magic in his veins. You’ll feel the weight, the urgency, the pain. It hits, even if it’s your first time stepping into the Cosmere.



But if you have? You’ll feel it in your bones.



Every passing reference, every myth-name dropped, every strange ability—each one is a thread connecting to a far larger tapestry. And once the final name is spoken, once the mask slips, you’ll find yourself looking back at the entire story with new eyes.



You’ll want to reread it. You’ll have to. And when you do, you’ll realize: the clues were always there.



This is Sanderson giving long-time fans a mirror: not just a story about the Cosmere, but about what the Cosmere is becoming.



Supporting This Journey



If this review helped you, made you feel something, or got you even more excited about the Cosmere, I’d love your support. I write and create because I believe in stories that dig deep—stories like The Sunlit Man that leave a mark.



You can support my work in a few ways:
Buy me a coffee on Ko-fi,
Join my Patreon community for deeper content and behind-the-scenes thoughts,
or subscribe to my Substack to follow everything I’m creating and sharing.



Every bit of support helps me keep reading, writing, and connecting stories like these to the people who care about them. Thanks for staying in the light.

Title: The Frugal Wizard’s Handbook for Surviving Medieval England (Secret Projects, #2)
Author: Brandon Sanderson
My Review:
The Frugal Wizard’s Handbook for Surviving Medieval England by Brandon Sanderson
Book Review

A Misunderstood, Hilarious, Heartfelt Ride
Brandon Sanderson’s *The Frugal Wizard’s Handbook for Surviving Medieval England* isn’t the book most people expected it to be—and that’s exactly why I loved it. It’s strange, self-aware, and filled with a charm that feels like a breath of fresh air. Yes, the reviews have been mixed. And no, Frugal Wizard doesn’t follow the typical narrative structures of *Stormlight Archive* or the tight magic of *Mistborn*. But if you can approach this book on its own terms, what you’ll find is a delightfully oddball adventure that works because of its quirks, not in spite of them. I loved it!

Let’s address the elephant in the room: a lot of readers were disappointed. And in many ways, that disappointment isn’t about what the book is—it’s about what it *isn’t*. Fans expecting a deep, sprawling fantasy epic with lore-rich maps, hard magic systems, and epic stakes were left scratching their heads. This book doesn’t pretend to be that. It’s playful. It’s punchy. It pokes fun while casually jumping between dimensions and eras. It doesn’t build a detailed world so much as sketch it in with absurdity and background jokes. If you expected high fantasy, this might feel like a prank. But if you walk in ready to be entertained, it’s a blast. And I mean, wow, this was so much fun!

Embracing the Chaos
What I found so refreshing about *Frugal Wizard* was its sheer willingness to be different. The protagonist, who does experience amnesia, it still works. But I believe those who disagree with that, have been burned in the past before. The protagonist (who does have a name.. I won’t spoil for you) becomes a fascinating first-person to walk through this story with! He explores identity, redemption, and purpose. The “handbook” excerpts themselves are fun. clever, ridiculous, and oddly insightful. You really need to pay attention to them!

This book reminded me that Sanderson is more than just a builder of intricate worlds. He’s also a storyteller who knows how to have fun. *Frugal Wizard* feels like Sanderson giving himself permission to play, to experiment, and to write without the weight of world-ending stakes. And there’s something deeply enjoyable about watching a master of structure write something gleefully structure-less.

The Joy of Low-Stakes, High-Concept Fantasy
Not every book needs to change your life. Some books are meant to entertain you, surprise you, and sneak a little meaning in along the way. That’s what this book does. It’s funny and fast, but it also asks a few meaningful questions about who we are when everything familiar is stripped away. It’s not world-shattering, but it’s definitely worth the ride.

In some ways, I think *Frugal Wizard* will age better than many of Sanderson’s “mainstream” works. As expectations fade and readers start to encounter it on its own terms, I suspect it’ll find a second life as a favorite—beloved by those who weren’t afraid to enjoy it for what it is.

Final Thoughts
Is this book for everyone? Probably not. But if you’re tired of taking fantasy too seriously, or if you’ve ever wished your portal fantasy came with a satirical instruction manual, then this book is for you. I went in with an open mind, and I came out smiling.

Brandon Sanderson didn’t write another epic here. He wrote something rare: a book that makes fun of itself without losing its heart. And in a sea of overly serious fantasy, that feels like magic. Go Sanderson!

Title: Oathbringer (The Stormlight Archive, #3)
Author: Brandon Sanderson
My Review:
2025.07.08
It would be disingenuous to write this review as if I've never read Oathbringer before. I have, in fact, read Oathbringer THREE TIMES NOW! So, with this review, I will be looking at **Dalinar and the Challenge of Redemption**. This will be a first for me, in reviewing a specific character for an entire review. You may read my previous reviews here: (https://www.mattbramer.com/posts/2022-11-29-bookreview-oathbringer-brandonsanderson.html)

Spoilers ahead! If you haven’t read Brandon Sanderson’s Stormlight Archive, up to and including Oathbringer, beware!

## "You cannot have my pain."

These five words mark the climax of *Oathbringer*, the third installment in Brandon Sanderson's *Stormlight Archive*. One of the most significant character transformations in the entire Stormlight Archive—which is saying something, because every character here is broken and burdened, in need of redemption. With Dalinar, that is no exception.

These words, are simple, yet dense. In that moment, Dalinar Kholin doesn't defeat Odium with a weapon, he wins by refusing to surrender the very thing that defines his fall: his pain.

Dalinar's arc isn't a typical redemption story. It's not about moving on from past mistakes. It's about standing in the middle of them—broken, ashamed, remembered—and still choosing to become someone new. It’s about owning the wreckage, not being owned by it.

And yet, his journey also raises profound questions. What does it mean to change? Can someone truly become good after doing so much evil? And if your transformation is *aided*—perhaps even *orchestrated*—by a godlike being, does that make it less authentic?

And here’s where we shift from the Cosmere: **In our real world, that answer is yes, a thousand times yes.**

## Real Redemption: A Gospel Lens

In the world of Roshar, Dalinar’s redemption is hard-won. But in our world, the truth is even more staggering: **redemption isn’t earned—it’s given.** Freely.

As Paul writes in *Romans 3:22–24* (LSB):

'even the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all those who believe; for there is no distinction; for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, being justified as a gift by His grace through the redemption which is in Christ Jesus; ' **Romans 3:22-24**

That’s real grace. We don’t change ourselves and then come to God—He redeems us *while* we’re broken. He *remembers* our sin, yet justifies us anyway.

Dalinar’s struggle resonates so deeply because it echoes this truth: **redemption requires ownership**. But in Christ, it also requires surrender—to grace, not guilt.

That’s reality, in Christ. But with the Stormlight Archive, this is the tension at the heart of Dalinar's journey: **redemption through accountability… and manipulation.** Because there is always another secret.

## Roshar’s Tension: Accountability and Manipulation

Back in the Stormlight Archive, Dalinar’s arc balances two forces: **personal accountability and divine manipulation.** As Brandon Sanderson would write, that’s the tension that pulses through *Oathbringer*. Cultivation doesn’t just encourage Dalinar—she *alters* him. She removes the memory of Evi’s death, creating a space where he can begin to grow. Only later, when he’s stronger, does she let him remember.

So the question becomes: Is this growth *authentic*? Or is it *engineered*?

This is the line Sanderson invites us to walk: **Dalinar’s redemption was real—but not unaided.** In the same way, we don’t become new people by ourselves. We need outside intervention. And for us, that intervention isn’t a Shard—it’s a Savior.

## The Weight Dalinar Carried

Before he became the Bondsmith. Before the Stormfather. Before the unity. Dalinar Kholin was the **Blackthorn**—a warlord of unrelenting fury and unmatched brutality. He slaughtered his way to conquest. He tore through enemies with a primal hunger. He once drank himself numb so he wouldn't have to face what he had done. And worst of all **(HUGE SPOILER)**, he burned the city of Rathalas and incinerated its people—including his own wife, Evi.

*This is not a man easily redeemed.*

His transformation begins with memory loss. A gift—or a curse—granted by the Nightwatcher (Cultivation). She removes the memory of Evi from his mind, pruning his pain so that he might grow into something new. **Only later, after he has begun to change, does the full memory return. The fire. The screams. The truth.**

This is the backdrop for one of the most painful and powerful arcs in all of fantasy. Dalinar must become a better man *before* he knows just how broken he was. And when he finally remembers? He doesn't crumble. He doesn’t excuse. He doesn’t deflect. He *stands* in it. **“I will take responsibility for what I have done. If I must fall, I will rise each time a better man.”**

That’s the essence of Dalinar’s arc. He’s not redeemed because he forgets his sins. He’s redeemed because he remembers—and still chooses to fight for something more.

## Cultivation’s Hand

But here’s where things get complicated. **Cultivation is a Shard.** She represents change, growth, pruning for purpose. She sees Dalinar’s potential and removes a key piece of his burden so he can reach it. She creates a space for transformation.

So… is Dalinar’s change real? Can you take credit for becoming a better person if someone *helped* you forget how bad you once were? This is the question of *Oathbringer*. Cultivation *chose* Dalinar. But he still had to walk the path. **He had to endure the shame of rediscovered memory.** He had to choose *not* to give his pain to Odium, who offered him escape and absolution. **He had to *own* his pain.** Sanderson doesn’t let us settle into easy answers. He makes us sit in the discomfort.

## The Moment He Refused to Break

The confrontation with Odium is perhaps the most thematically loaded scene in the entire Stormlight Archive. And my goodness—it’s massive. Point-of-views crashing across Thaylen Field, epic prose, a wild battle... everything firing at once. Odium offers Dalinar exactly what he wants: relief, freedom, release from guilt. From pain. From the memory of screams. **All he has to do is give it up. Let Odium take it.** Let the Shard of passion and fury and righteous indignation *own* his agony.

But Dalinar doesn’t. He says no. **“You cannot have my pain.”**

It’s one of the most powerful lines Sanderson has ever written. Because pain, here, is *not* a curse. It’s not something to be avoided or eliminated. **It is the *proof* of change. The *proof* of growth.** Dalinar’s pain is not his punishment—**it is his reminder**. It keeps him honest. It keeps him humble. And in that refusal, he reclaims not just himself… but something larger. He opens a Perpendicularity. He becomes the Bondsmith in full. **He unites.** That is how he defeats Odium—not with steel or Shards, but with truth.

## A Different Kind of Hero

Dalinar isn’t Kaladin. He’s not the brooding, noble, tragic hero. He’s not Shallan, trying to reconcile fragments of self. He’s not Adolin, trying to prove himself in the shadow of others.

**Dalinar is a war criminal. A man rebuilt. A man haunted.**

And that’s what makes him the heart of *Oathbringer*. His redemption doesn’t make him perfect—it makes him *present*. He leads not because he’s clean, but because he *knows* the stakes of failure. He unites not because he’s a shining beacon, but because he’s lived through the darkness.

He is the embodiment of the First Ideal: **"Life before death. Strength before weakness. Journey before destination."**

Dalinar chooses life when death seemed easier. **He shows strength through surrender.** And he commits to the journey, even when the destination remains uncertain.

## Final Thoughts: The Immortal Words

This is where Sanderson turns the lens back on us. Dalinar’s arc doesn’t offer easy answers. It offers a challenge. **It says: *Look at your pain. Don’t run from it. Don’t hand it off. Face it. Own it. And let it shape you into something better.*** Amazing.

*Oathbringer* isn’t about saving the world—it’s about saving a man. A man who nearly destroyed everything he touched. **A man who, in the end, refused to be defined by what he had done.**

A man who became something more because he *chose* to bear the weight. I’ll never forget the feeling of reading that scene for the first time. The crackle of the Perpendicularity. The Stormlight. The unity. The power of pain reclaimed. It’s why Dalinar Kholin is my favorite character in the Cosmere. Not because he was always strong. But because he learned what strength really means. **"Journey before destination."**

Title: John Quincy Adams
Author: Harlow Giles Unger
My Review:
John Quincy Adams by Harlow Giles Unger
Book Review

Intro
This is a solid biography—well-researched, well-written, and surprisingly readable for covering such a dense and important life. Harlow Giles Unger doesn't just tell the story of a former president—he revives a figure who’s often brushed over in American history books but was, in truth, one of the most brilliant and service-minded leaders this country has ever had. Truth be told, I often skip over John Quincy Adams as I recount the US Presidents. After reading this, I will not forget JQA!

Prose
Unger’s writing is clean and confident. He brings energy to Adams’s life without over-dramatizing (which can certainly happen elsewhere). The narrative is chronological, accessible, and always moving forward—you never feel bogged down in academic details. Good stuff!

Early Life
As a boy, John Quincy Adams (JQA) witnessed the American Revolution firsthand and even traveled with his father on diplomatic missions. He kept journals from age 12 and studied at Harvard, where he developed the fierce intellect and moral clarity that would define him. This is one of the fullest eye-witness accounts we have of the Revolution, I may read his diary as it's out there for reading!

Career
JQA is, without a doubt, an underappreciated president. That becomes clear very quickly as you walk through his life. He was the son of a president—John Adams—and carried the weight of that legacy with grace and integrity. But JQA didn’t coast on his father’s name. He built his own legacy, through intellect, moral conviction, and service.

He was already a diplomat by his teens, serving in foreign posts and even acting as secretary to the U.S. minister in Russia. He mastered multiple languages, including Latin, Greek, French, German, and Dutch. George Washington himself called him the most valuable public servant in America. How many people can say that?

JQA was the "principal architect" of the Monroe Doctrine as Secretary of State under President James Monroe. He believed deeply in American independence and moral leadership on the world stage, but also in restraint and justice. He thought long-term and acted with principle.

Presidency
And then came the presidency. He won in what was essentially a four-way brawl—an election so messy it had to be decided in the House of Representatives (and gave rise to claims of a “corrupt bargain” when Henry Clay, Speaker of the House, supported Adams and was later named Secretary of State). That charge followed him for the rest of his term, even though there’s no solid evidence it was anything more than politics as usual.

He only served one term, but not for lack of vision. His presidency was packed with forward-thinking ideas: national infrastructure, a national university, exploration and scientific advancement, even funding for the arts. He believed the federal government had a role to play in strengthening the country’s future—not just economically, but intellectually and morally. He pushed for roads, canals, schools, even a national observatory.

But, as easy as that sounds, it was difficult for JQA to accomplish his ideas. Populism—the political movement that champions the “common man” over elites—was on the rise, and Jackson’s supporters painted JQA as elitist and out of touch. Worse, he refused to play the political game—he wouldn’t hand out jobs to allies or clean house of his enemies. He governed by principle, not popularity, and in Washington, that came at a cost. Still, reading about it now, it’s clear: a lot of what he proposed was ahead of its time. He saw what America could become, and tried to steer it in that direction—even when no one followed.

Real Life
Here’s the thing: Unger doesn’t shy away from the political losses, the frustrations, or the personal costs. But that’s what makes JQA admirable. He didn’t bend just to win. He spoke the truth, stood for what was right, and often paid the price. And then, incredibly, his story doesn't end with the presidency. And what fascinated me was, this book doesn't spend too much time on the Presidency. This is more about the man, than the office. As is most often about each President.

After Presidency
After leaving the White House, most former presidents retire quietly. Not JQA. He went back into public service—this time in the House of Representatives—where he served for another seventeen years! He became a relentless opponent of slavery and the “gag rule” that tried to prevent anti-slavery petitions from being heard in Congress. He fought for what was right, even when it made him deeply unpopular. Even when he stood nearly alone.

Faith and Character
And something I thought was incredible: JQA read the Bible every year. He journaled, reflected, and committed himself to spiritual growth and moral clarity. He believed that liberty and virtue were inseparable. In a world where political ambition so often crowds out personal character, Adams was a man who kept both in view.

He even served as president of the American Bible Society, a role that fit him perfectly. He believed the Bible was not only foundational for personal morality, but also for the health of the republic itself. For JQA, the Scriptures were more than religious reading—they were a civic compass.

His Death
He literally died on the House floor—suffering a stroke in the Capitol while in the middle of serving his country. His last words were, “This is the end of earth, but I am content.” Whoa!

Final Thoughts
If you’re looking for a biography that goes beyond just the office of the presidency—if you want to understand what it looks like to live a life of principle, service, discipline, and faith—John Quincy Adams is worth your time. Another great American Presidential Biography complete! That's the seventh biography that I've read now! Washington, Adams, Jefferson, Madison, Monroe, Grant, and now JQA!

Title: Amazing Grace in the Life of William Wilberforce
Author: John      Piper
My Review:
Wow—what a life. Piper writes beautifully (this is the first book I’ve read by him!), and this little book really opened my eyes to a man who was both deeply joyful and deeply committed to Christ. The “amazing grace” in the title isn’t just about saving grace—it’s about sustaining grace. Grace that fueled Wilberforce through sickness, setbacks, and decades of work to end the slave trade.

One thing that stood out was how happy he was. Not fake-happy. Real, deep, Christ-centered joy. Piper describes him as “always happy,” and it’s true. Wilberforce embodied what Piper calls Christian Hedonism—the idea that delighting in God is the heart of the Christian life. That joy gave him strength, perspective, and perseverance.

Piper also draws from Wilberforce’s own book, A Practical View of Christianity. Wilberforce didn’t want a cold, lifeless faith. He pushed back hard against the cultural Christianity of his day—people who said the right things but weren’t changed by grace. For him, true faith wasn’t just belief—it was joy in Christ that spilled over into action, especially in fighting injustice.

This book encouraged me more than I expected. I came into it knowing almost nothing about Wilberforce, and I left feeling like I had met a man who truly walked with Jesus. It stirred up in me a desire to live more joyfully—not just to have joy, but to live from it. It made me want to stay faithful in the daily grind, to keep going when things are hard, and to let the grace of God shape everything I do. (And Piper does all this in only 80 pages!)

And just to be clear—despite the title, this book isn’t about the hymn Amazing Grace. It’s about the actual grace of God that saved Wilberforce and carried him through his whole life. That grace gave him perseverance in politics, gentleness in suffering, and a contagious gladness that came straight from knowing Christ. Piper captures that beautifully.

Title: Children of Dune (Dune #3)
Author: Frank Herbert
My Review:
Children of Dune by Frank Herbert
Book Review

Started Reading 2025.06.19. Finished Reading 2025.06.30.

Oh man. Children of Dune absolutely blew me away.

Plot
This book is tight. It’s everything I love about Dune — the mysticism, the politics, the harsh beauty of Arrakis — but turned up a notch. It's like the desert winds blew the veil off the future, and Frank Herbert walked us straight into something ancient, prophetic, and terrifyingly beautiful.

The story picks up after Dune Messiah, with Paul gone, Muad'Dib is now legend, and his children — Leto II and Ghanima — at the center of a whirlwind of prophecy, power, and fear. But what makes Children of Dune so awesome isn't just the plot (which is killer) — it’s the secrets. We go places. Secret places. Hidden tunnels beneath Sietches. Hidden layers of consciousness. Hidden motives wrapped in religious fervor and political ambition. It’s secrets on secrets — and every time one’s revealed, it leads to something even deeper. I mean, this is incredible. My brain breaks and I love it. The combination of words that describe tri-ocular view that opens the veils of time... ahhh, so good.

The Golden Path
The book grapples with destiny in such a unique way, and really the entire Dune series does. Leto II begins to glimpse what’s called the Golden Path, and without spoiling too much — wow. It's heavy, it’s philosophical, and it’s epic in the truest sense. He’s not just making decisions for the present; he’s making choices that will shape humanity for thousands of years. And yet it never feels too abstract, again, it's awesome. It's haunting. Awesome.

The World
Arrakis just keeps getting better and better in these books. It’s not just a desert anymore — it feels alive. You can sense the changes happening, the tension between the old ways and the new. Fremen who once survived on spice and ritual now talk about planting trees and changing the land. And you can tell not everyone’s on board. The land itself almost feels like it’s resisting.

I loved all the small details — the worms, the sietch tunnels, the smell of spice in the air. Herbert doesn’t just describe a planet; he builds a whole spiritual ecosystem. It’s beautiful, dangerous, and sacred. You feel like if you took one step in the wrong direction, the planet might swallow you whole. It makes everything feel bigger, like the stakes aren’t just political or personal — they’re planetary.

Pre-Born
The whole “pre-born” concept is wild. Leto and Ghanima aren’t just kids — they’ve got all the memories of their ancestors. Literally thousands of lives in their heads. That alone is nuts, but the way they live with it is what got me. Every conversation, every decision they make — it’s like they’re filtering it through the wisdom (and trauma) of generations. And yet they’re still trying to be themselves.

There’s this tension between holding onto who they are and not getting lost in the crowd inside their minds. Alia failed that test. Leto and Ghanima are constantly on the edge of losing themselves too. That makes even the small moments feel intense. I kept thinking how lonely that must be — to never truly have a thought that’s just your own. And yet, they carry it like it’s normal. It’s incredible.

Jessica Atreides
I really liked Jessica in this one. She’s not just back to be the wise mom — she’s sharp. Suspicious. Strategic. And at the same time, she cares about Leto and Ghanima in a way that feels genuine. You can tell she’s still carrying the weight of everything that happened with Paul, with the Bene Gesserit, and now she’s got to walk this tightrope between loyalty to her order and love for her family.

What I appreciated most is how Jessica sees through Alia right away. She knows something’s off. She doesn’t play games — she moves carefully, but she’s always ten steps ahead. It reminded me that in the Dune universe, the older generation still has a big part to play. Jessica’s presence adds this quiet pressure to the whole story, like she’s the last person who really remembers what it all cost.

Alia
And Alia... her story hit me hard. Watching her unravel was like watching a fire slowly consume something beautiful. She’s torn apart from the inside by the memories and the Baron’s voice — it’s terrifying and tragic. She knows what’s happening to her, and that makes it worse. Her descent into Abomination stands in direct contrast to Leto’s rise, and it makes the stakes so much more real. It’s one of the saddest arcs in the book, and it stayed with me.

Leto II
This book also has one of the most chilling and fascinating transformations I’ve ever read. Leto II… man, what a character. His journey is both monstrous and messianic. By the end, I was floored. What he becomes. What he embraces. It’s not just sci-fi — it’s mythic. AWESOME. I have heard about what happens to Leto II in this series, and that's what I'm racing towards, and we are getting "a beginning"... I love it!

Every character feels like they're standing on the edge of something greater than themselves, and watching them wrestle with that tension is just... awesome.

Prose
And can we talk about the writing? Herbert’s prose is dense, sure, but once you're locked in, it's hypnotic. That's the best word for it. I'm a little entranced as if I've taken on the melange! There's a rhythm to the way he reveals thoughts, layers of prescience, inner conflict, and theological depth.

Final Thoughts
In short: Children of Dune is incredible. If Dune planted the seeds, Children of Dune is the desert bloom that grows out of it: strange, fierce, and unforgettable. This is one of those books I’ll come back to again and again. I loved it. I love the Dune series. And with this one, I can feel the future opening wide — strange, golden, and full of purpose.

Title: Ahsoka (Star Wars)
Author: E.K. Johnston
My Review:
Ahsoka by E.K. Johnston
Book Review

Rating: 3/5 Stars

Intro
E.K. Johnston's "Ahsoka" occupies that peculiar middle ground where a book isn't quite good enough to recommend enthusiastically, yet isn't poor enough to dismiss outright. It's the literary equivalent of a shrug—competent, serviceable, but ultimately forgettable.

The Good
The book's strongest moments come early. The prologue featuring Ahsoka's confrontation with Maul crackles with tension and showcases Johnston's ability to capture the weight of these characters' shared history. Similarly, Ahsoka's internal monologue in the opening chapters provides genuine insight into her mindset following her departure from the Jedi Order. These glimpses into her psychological state—the doubt, the determination, the careful balance between her past and uncertain future—represent the novel at its most compelling.

One aspect that deserves particular praise is the audiobook production. Ashley Eckstein, who voices Ahsoka in the animated series, brings an authenticity to the narration that elevates the entire experience. The added sound effects and musical cues create an immersive atmosphere that makes even the slower sections more engaging. If you're going to experience this story, the audiobook is definitely the way to go.

The timeline placement is another strength worth noting. Set in the immediate aftermath of Order 66, the story captures Ahsoka during one of the most pivotal and traumatic periods in Star Wars history. There's something refreshing about seeing this galaxy-shattering event through the eyes of someone who was already on the outside looking in. The rawness of a galaxy where the Jedi have just been wiped out and the Empire is consolidating power provides a compelling backdrop that gives weight to even mundane moments.

For readers deeply invested in The Clone Wars animated series, these character beats likely resonate even more strongly. Johnston clearly understands Ahsoka as a character, and when she focuses on the former Padawan's internal journey, the writing finds its footing.

The Frustrating
Unfortunately, those strong character moments are buried beneath layers of mundane world-building and plotting that feels more obligatory than organic. Extended sequences devoted to board games that supposedly serve the larger plot left me checking the page count. The agricultural equipment descriptions and other minutiae that other reviewers have noted bog down what should be a tighter narrative about a character finding her place in a galaxy in turmoil.

The secondary characters—entirely new to someone like me who's only dabbled in The Clone Wars—fail to capture interest or feel essential to Ahsoka's story. They exist more as plot devices than fully realized people, making their extensive screen time feel unearned.

Perhaps most jarring is a brief cameo appearance by Princess Leia that feels completely disconnected from the story's flow. While only lasting a few paragraphs, it serves as an unwelcome reminder that you're reading a Star Wars product rather than experiencing Ahsoka's journey. These kinds of forced connections to the larger universe feel more like corporate mandate than organic storytelling.

The unnamed world where much of the action takes place similarly lacks the lived-in quality that makes the best Star Wars locations memorable. It's functional but forgettable, providing a backdrop rather than becoming a character in its own right.

The Verdict
"Ahsoka" feels like a book written primarily for completionists—those who need to know what happened to Ahsoka between her Clone Wars exit and her Rebels appearance. For that specific audience, it likely succeeds in filling narrative gaps and providing closure.

For everyone else, it's a perfectly adequate Star Wars novel that never quite justifies its existence beyond franchise maintenance. Johnston has the writing chops to deliver something special, as evidenced by those early chapters, but the book too often gets trapped in its own perceived obligations to be more than just Ahsoka's story.

It's not a bad book. It's just not a particularly necessary one.

Recommended for: Die-hard Ahsoka fans, Clone Wars completionists, but Skip if: You're looking for your next great Star Wars read or hoping for an accessible entry point to the character.

Title: Reformation Heroes Volume Two: 1522 - 1629 John Calvin, Theodore Beza, The Anabaptists, and many more
Author: Diana Kleyn
My Review:
Reformation Heroes Volume Two By Diana Kleyn and Joel R. Beeke
Book Review

If Volume One got you hooked on the drama, depth, and conviction of the Reformation, Volume Two will remind you why it mattered—and why it still does. Diana Kleyn and Joel Beeke return with another round of gripping, accessible biographies that breathe life into the lesser-known but no-less-heroic figures who carried the Reformation flame into the next generation.

Like the first volume, this one doesn’t read like a textbook—it reads like a storybook for grown-ups who love church history. Every chapter is a miniature drama of conviction, courage, and costly obedience. You’re not bogged down with names and dates. Instead, you're pulled into the personal, the human—the moments that shaped not just theology but lives and nations.

So, who are the heroes this time around?

We meet Guido de Brès, the author of the Belgic Confession, who wrote one of the most beautiful summaries of Reformed theology from a prison cell—then sealed it with his life. There’s Caspar Olevianus and Zacharias Ursinus, two young theologians who helped craft the Heidelberg Catechism, a document that still comforts and teaches centuries later. And behind them stood Elector Frederick III, the ruler whose support made it all possible.

Then there’s John Knox, the Scottish firebrand who preached with such force that even queens trembled. Jeanne d’Albret, Queen of Navarre, brings a refreshing contrast—quiet, regal courage as she defended the Huguenots in France. Jan Laski, a Polish reformer, traveled and led across Europe, tirelessly working for unity among Protestants. These stories stretch the borders of the Reformation we usually hear about, reminding us it wasn’t just a German or Swiss movement—it was international, and it was unstoppable.

And once again, Kleyn and Beeke bring out what made these people tick—not just what they did, but why they did it. These weren't just thinkers or rebels. They were people captured by the gospel, convinced that God's Word was worth living—and dying—for.

What makes these stories even more remarkable is the historical backdrop they played out against. While reformers were risking everything to preach the gospel and teach sound doctrine, the Roman Catholic Church was digging in its heels through the Council of Trent (1545–1563). This was Rome’s official answer to the Reformation—a hardline reaffirmation of its doctrines, sacraments, and authority. The council doubled down on the very issues reformers were confronting: indulgences, justification, Scripture, and the role of the Church. So these weren’t just personal risks—these believers were standing firm in the face of a global theological standoff. The stakes couldn’t have been higher.

Volume Two is all about perseverance. It shows how the Reformation didn’t just explode and then fade—it spread and deepened, passed on by faithful believers in pulpits, palaces, and prisons. The writing is crisp and clear, the pacing tight, and the stories full of soul. You finish each chapter both moved and motivated.

If you enjoyed Volume One, this is an easy yes. And if you're just starting your journey into the world of Reformation history, this volume stands just as strong on its own. It’s history with a heartbeat—and these are heroes worth knowing.

Title: The Case for Christ: A Journalist's Personal Investigation of the Evidence for Jesus (Case for ... Series)
Author: Lee Strobel
My Review:
The Case for Christ by Lee Strobel
Book Review

Started Reading 2025.06.03
Finished Reading 2025.06.10

I picked up The Case for Christ knowing the premise, and knowing the general story: non-believing journalist tries to conclude that Christianity is false and Jesus didn't rise from the dead, and turns out Christianity is true and Jesus did rise from the dead. Still, not knowing exactly what to expect, and honestly, it blew me away. This book is fantastic—scholarly without being dry, well-written, and packed with solid research that actually makes you want to dig deeper into the citations.

Lee Strobel, a former Chicago Tribune journalist, basically treats the question of Jesus like a massive investigative story. He interviews real scholars—not just random people with opinions, but actual historians, archaeologists, and biblical experts from serious universities. The guy knows how to ask the right questions and present complex stuff in a way that doesn't make your brain hurt.

The research is impressive. These aren't fluff interviews; Strobel is talking to people who've spent their careers studying ancient texts, digging up archaeological evidence, and analyzing historical documents. And the citations? Gold mine. I'm already planning to chase down several of the sources he references. This book is filled with tabs now, for research and evangelism!

The book reads like good journalism, which makes sense given Strobel's background. He knows how to tell a story and keep you engaged while delivering serious information. No academic jargon that makes you feel like you need a PhD to follow along—just clear, solid writing that respects your intelligence.

However, one thing that got a bit old: every chapter starts with some legal case that's supposed to parallel the evidence he's about to discuss. It's a clever idea, and it works great at first, but by chapter six or seven, you're like "alright, I get it, we're building a case." It's not a dealbreaker, just a little repetitive. The actual content that follows is always worth it and really what I'm trying to get to instead of the introductions.

This is the kind of book that changes how you think about historical evidence and religious claims. Whether you're a believer looking for intellectual grounding or someone curious about what scholars actually think about Jesus, it delivers. The fact that Strobel provides all those citations shows he's confident in his sources and wants you to keep learning.

The Case for Christ is exactly what good popular scholarship should be—serious research presented in a way that's actually enjoyable to read. The minor repetition with the legal introductions doesn't hurt what's ultimately a really solid, well-researched book that takes both its subject and its readers seriously.

If you're interested in Christian apologetics, historical Jesus research, or just like books that make you smarter while keeping you engaged, this one's a winner. Plus, those citations will keep you busy for months if you really want to dive deep.

Title: Thrawn (Star Wars: Thrawn, #1)
Author: Timothy Zahn
My Review:
Thrawn by Timothy Zahn
Book Review

Started Reading: 2025.06.02
Finished Reading: 2025.06.09

Timothy Zahn's Thrawn delivers exactly what Star Wars fans have been craving: a character-driven story that feels both fresh and authentically part of the galaxy far, far away. For newcomers to the blue-skinned Chiss strategist, this novel serves as the perfect introduction to one of the franchise's most compelling antagonists.

Set between Episodes III and IV and connecting to the Rebels animated series, Thrawn fills in crucial backstory while standing entirely on its own merits. I've only ever read the canon entries: Mike Chen's Brotherhood and Chuck Wendig's Aftermath, this book is a different but equally compelling approach to Star Wars storytelling.

Zahn's greatest strength is in his ability to make Thrawn simultaneously alien and relatable. The Chiss officer's methodical rise through Imperial ranks unfolds with surgical precision, each calculated move revealing layers of tactical brilliance that made him a fan favorite in the first place. It is not easy to make a non-human feel human.

Zahn's writing style mirrors Thrawn —measured, analytical, almost clinical in its precision. This deliberate approach may feel less fun than typical Star Wars prose, but it perfectly captures Thrawn's calculating nature and creates a unique reading experience that sets this book apart from other entries in the expanded universe.

The supporting cast provides necessary depth, though some character arcs feel deliberately uncomfortable. This isn't a flaw but rather Zahn's commentary on the corrupting nature of Imperial service. Watching certain characters make choices that compromise their values creates genuine tension, even when we understand the larger forces at play.

What makes Thrawn particularly satisfying is how it balances political intrigue with the adventure elements that define great Star Wars storytelling. The pacing never drags, and Zahn's understanding of Imperial bureaucracy adds strength without overwhelming the narrative.

This book succeeds as both a standalone story and foundation for deeper exploration of one of Star Wars' most fascinating characters. Thrawn reminds us why Timothy Zahn remains essential reading for any serious fan of the Star Wars expanded universe.

Title: A Calamity of Souls
Author: David Baldacci
My Review:
A Calamity of Souls by David Baldacci
Book Review

This was my first David Baldacci novel—and actually my first mystery novel ever—and I’m still kind of reeling from how good it was.

I picked up A Calamity of Souls on a whim, not really sure what I was getting into, and by the end I was totally immersed. I didn’t expect to care this deeply about a legal mystery set in the 1960s South, but Baldacci makes it impossible not to. The story is gripping, yes, but it’s also deeply human and layered in ways I didn’t expect. I wasn’t just following clues—I was feeling every conversation, every injustice, every bit of tension in the air.

The characters really got to me. Jack Lee is such a complicated figure—he’s not a hero in the traditional sense, but he’s trying to do the right thing in a place and time that makes that incredibly hard. Desiree DuBose? She’s a force. Smart, steady, brave. Their dynamic isn’t flashy, but it’s honest, and it carried so much weight. I appreciated how their partnership developed without ever feeling forced. It felt earned.

The mystery itself is strong—it unfolds in a way that kept me fully engaged, and it didn’t rely on cheap twists or dramatic gimmicks. Instead, it felt real. Messy, tense, painful at times, but real. And the setting—the American South during the Civil Rights era—adds so much more than just atmosphere. It’s central to the story. The racism, the fear, the silence, the complicated relationships—it’s all there, and it’s handled with weight and care.

More than once, I found myself just sitting with a line or a scene, letting it sink in. There’s something haunting about the way Baldacci writes this particular story. You feel the hopelessness and the resistance. You feel the cost of truth. But you also feel these small moments of hope, of connection, of people quietly choosing courage.

I came for a mystery and found something much deeper—something that challenged me, moved me, and made me think. I didn’t expect that. And now, I kind of want everyone I know to read this book so we can talk about it.

Title: Reformation Heroes Volume One: 1140 - 1572 Martin Luther, William Tyndale, John Knox and many more
Author: Diana Kleyn
Additional Authors: Joel R. Beeke, David Cochran Heath
My Review:
Reformation Heroes Volume One by Diana Kleyn & Joel R. Beeke
Book Review

This isn't your dusty seminary textbook—it's a gripping collection of biographical snapshots that transforms 16th-century reformers into flesh-and-blood heroes you'll actually want to know.

Kleyn and Beeke have pulled off something remarkable here. They've taken figures who often feel like marble statues in church history and made them breathe again. Each profile reads like a tightly crafted short story, complete with the drama, struggle, and triumph that marked these extraordinary lives.

The writing strikes the perfect balance—scholarly enough to respect the material, accessible enough that you don't need a theology degree to follow along. The authors clearly did their homework, but they wear their research lightly. Instead of drowning you in dates and theological minutiae, they focus on the human elements that make these stories unforgettable.

The biographical sketches are perfectly sized. Long enough to give you real substance, short enough to keep you turning pages. Whether you're reading about Luther's bold stand at Worms or Calvin's transformation of Geneva, each account builds to genuine moments of tension and resolution.

Who are the Reformation Heroes of this book?
The cast of characters in Reformation Heroes Volume One reads like a who's who of gospel courage, each one a story waiting to be rediscovered.

It all kicks off with Peter Waldo, a rich merchant from Lyon who gave it all up to follow Christ and preach the Word—centuries before Luther nailed anything to a door. His movement, the Waldensians, faced brutal persecution, but their stubborn commitment to Scripture lit a fuse that never went out. Then there's John Wycliffe, the so-called "Morning Star of the Reformation," who defied the Church by translating the Bible into English and calling out hypocrisy from the pulpit to the papacy. He didn’t start the Reformation, but he set the stage.

Anne of Bohemia doesn’t get as much spotlight, but she played a quiet, crucial role. As queen of England, she protected Wycliffe and helped spread his ideas across Europe. One of the places those ideas landed was Bohemia, where John Huss picked up the torch. Huss preached Christ boldly, stood against corruption, and paid for it with his life. Burned at the stake, his death became a rallying cry for reformers a hundred years later.

Of course, you can’t talk about the Reformation without Martin Luther. The monk who sparked a movement by challenging Rome’s abuses and reclaiming the gospel of grace. His defiance at Worms is legendary, but it’s his deep wrestling with Scripture and conscience that makes him unforgettable. Beside him stood Philip Melanchthon, Luther’s quieter counterpart—brilliant, kind, and instrumental in shaping Reformation doctrine through the Augsburg Confession. Their convictions found political backing in the Protest at Speyer (1529), where a group of German princes refused to bow to imperial pressure. That’s where the term “Protestant” was born.

Martin Bucer was the Reformation’s peacemaker. Serving in Strasbourg, he worked tirelessly to bring unity across theological lines. He didn’t get the headlines, but his influence stretched far—into England and beyond. Over in Zurich, Ulrich Zwingli was doing his own bold reforming, pushing for Scripture alone and clashing with Luther on the Lord’s Supper. His successor, Heinrich Bullinger, carried the flame with steady hands and clear teaching, helping to shape what would become mainstream Reformed theology.

England’s story takes a dramatic turn with William Tyndale, whose English Bible translation would echo through centuries. Hunted down and eventually martyred, his dying prayer—“Lord, open the King of England’s eyes”—would soon be answered. Enter King Edward VI, the boy-king who, during his short reign, moved the English Church decisively toward Protestantism. And standing behind much of that reform was Thomas Cranmer, the Archbishop of Canterbury, author of the Book of Common Prayer, and a man whose quiet strength carried the movement forward—even when it cost him his life.

Then there’s Hugh Latimer and Nicholas Ridley—two bishops, two martyrs, one unforgettable moment. As the flames rose around them, Latimer’s final words to Ridley rang out: “Be of good comfort… we shall this day light such a candle… as I trust shall never be put out.” That candle still burns.

What elevates this book is how it connects the dots between personal character and historical impact. These weren't just intellectual giants—they were people who faced real fears, made costly decisions, and often paid dearly for their convictions. The authors never shy away from their subjects' flaws, which makes their achievements all the more impressive.

If you've ever wondered what made the Reformation tick beyond the theological debates, this is your entry point. Kleyn and Beeke have created something rare: a history book that reads like a page-turner while delivering genuine insight into one of Christianity's most pivotal eras. Perfect for anyone who wants to understand not just what the reformers believed, but who they were as people. Volume One leaves you genuinely excited for what comes next.

I recommend this book for history buffs, students of the Reformation, anyone who appreciates well-told biographical narratives, and readers looking for inspiring examples of courage under pressure.

Title: James Monroe: A Life
Author: Tim  McGrath
My Review:
James Monroe by Tim McGrath
Book Review

Started Reading 2025.03.27
Finished Reading 2025.05.28

Tim McGrath’s James Monroe is a masterful and absorbing biography that brings to vivid life one of America's most underrated presidents. This is the sixth presidential biography I’ve read, and I continue to be amazed at how each one opens new dimensions to our early leaders. Monroe, in particular, turns out to be far more interesting and complex than I ever realized—and McGrath captures it all with clarity, insight, and elegance.

Monroe isn’t just a footnote between Madison and John Quincy Adams—he’s a pivotal figure in the shaping of the early republic. While often overshadowed by bigger names, Monroe held the nation together during one of its most fragile moments. His presidency, though marked by relative peace and prosperity, was a time of deep national tensions—regional divisions, the growing issue of slavery, and questions about the future of expansion. Monroe may not have been the loudest voice in history, but his steady, pragmatic leadership was exactly what America needed.

McGrath’s narrative is deeply engaging, blending detailed historical analysis with rich storytelling. From Monroe's dual role as Secretary of State and Secretary of War during the War of 1812 to the launch of the Monroe Doctrine, the book illustrates a man of deep commitment to national unity and republican ideals.

I was surprised by several remarkable details: Monroe had the first outdoor presidential inauguration. He began the reconstruction of the White House with just $281 after it was burned by the British. And though part of the so-called “Virginia Dynasty,” he felt distinct—more soldier than philosopher, more doer than dreamer.

The book also offers touching and humanizing moments, especially through the portrayal of his wife, Elizabeth Kortright Monroe. A refined and cosmopolitan woman, Elizabeth brought a sense of dignity and discretion to the White House, but her chronic illness often kept her out of the public eye. Their marriage was strong, and the affection between them shines through McGrath’s pages.

One of the most poignant facts I learned was about Monroe’s final resting place. Originally buried in New York after his death in 1831, his body was later exhumed and reinterred in Richmond, Virginia—so he could be laid to rest beside his beloved wife. That single act tells you so much about the man behind the public legacy.

McGrath handles Monroe’s contradictions with honesty and care, exploring his views on slavery, race, and expansion without simplifying or excusing. The book doesn't shy away from complexity—it embraces it, which makes it all the more rewarding.

James Monroe may be one of the most underappreciated figures in American history, but McGrath’s biography helps restore the respect he deserves. If you’re interested in American history, leadership, or the formation of a nation, James Monroe is a must-read. It’s one of the finest presidential biographies I’ve encountered—thoughtful, vivid, and just plain great storytelling.

Title: The Eye of the World (The Wheel of Time, #1)

The Eye of the World by Robert Jordan

Returning to The Eye of the World felt like coming home to something grand and familiar—yet still full of mystery. Even knowing where the story is headed, I was completely swept up again in the depth of the world, the richness of the characters, and the tension in every twist. Jordan’s storytelling is masterful, laying down the foundation for an epic while keeping the stakes intensely personal.

This time through, I found myself noticing more—the early hints, the worldbuilding depth, the character dynamics that I missed the first time because I was racing through pages just to see what happened next. There’s a rhythm to Jordan’s writing that draws you in and doesn't let go. Every page feels purposeful.

A lot of people compare this series to The Lord of the Rings, and yes, the influence is there—but The Wheel of Time quickly carves its own path. It’s not a copy—it’s a reimagining of epic fantasy on a massive scale, with its own voice and vision.

This book remains a must-read for fantasy fans. The magic system is fascinating, the world feels lived in, and the characters grow in real, meaningful ways. Whether you’re just starting the journey or coming back for a second (or third) time, The Eye of the World continues to deliver.

Title: Yumi and the Nightmare Painter (Hoid's Travails, #2)
Author: Brandon Sanderson
My Review:
Yumi and the Nightmare Painter by Brandon Sanderson
Book Review

Read: 2025.05.08

How does Brandon Sanderson keep doing this?

A fun, heartfelt, and intellectually satisfying read. Sanderson fans will be thrilled—and if you’ve never read his work before, this just might be the one to get you hooked.

Yumi and the Nightmare Painter is a beautifully strange and emotionally rich tale that could only come from the mind of Sanderson. This story is certainly set within the Cosmere universe, but it’s a one-off story that sits well next to the other great stories and series. This is personal, filled with heart, a great mystery, and just the right amount of weird.

Yumi and Painter are two of the most compelling characters Sanderson has written in a while. They're flawed (and that's coming from someone who doesn't care much about characters -- I more focus on the plot). Each of them is called to duty. With that comes trauma, however, they stay hopeful -- I really liked that.

Watching their Connection grow (emphasis on the capital "C")—across cultures, personalities, and even realms—is one of the most rewarding parts of the book. Their relationship is slow-burn, and it fits nicely into the entire narrative.

The worldbuilding, as usual, is top-tier. Though this story is more contained than some of his larger stories, it's still bursting with classic Sanderson touches—unique magic systems, compelling visual design, and under-the-surface Cosmere connections.

For readers familiar with his "Realmatic Theory", there’s a lot to chew on. Spiritwebs, Cognitive shadows, and other cosmere-wide concepts are present here both subtitlety and no so subtitlety and always satisfying. Hopefully it doesn't overwhelm those who aren't Sanderson readers.

And Hoid as the narrator? Absolutely delightful. His voice is perfect as the narrator. He is warm, witty, and just the right touch of fourth-wall-breaking charm. It’s one of Sanderson’s smartest storytelling choices in the book. He is whimsical and always so self-aware -- definitely not a distraction at times. I actually appreciate how at one point, Hoid does explains the most nuanced parts of the story. I can see how it wouldn't be good for some readers who are smarter than I, but I liked it.

The pacing is surprisingly tight—this is a relatively short book by Sanderson standards. Very good job, the way he structured this story. Everything builds toward an absolutely fantastic ending: emotional, well-earned, and beautifully executed. Seriously, the conclusion is great-great-great stuff. It ties together the emotional and thematic threads so well that I found myself just sitting with it afterward, soaking it in.

If you're new to the Cosmere, Yumi and the Nightmare Painter is a wonderful starting point—it's self-contained but still rich with hints that there’s a bigger universe out there. And if you're a longtime fan, there’s plenty to geek out about.

Title: The Path to Being a Pastor: A Guide for the Aspiring
Author: Bobby Jamieson
My Review:
The Path to Being a Pastor: A Guide for the Aspiring by Bobby Jamieson
Book Review

My goodness, 9Marks does not miss.

Every once in a while, a book comes along that I don't just learn from—it searches me. It holds up a mirror and asks deeper questions than I was prepared to answer. That’s exactly what Bobby Jamieson’s The Path to Being a Pastor: A Guide for the Aspiring does. This isn’t a manual for future ministers; it’s a spiritual gut check for anyone who feels even the faintest pull toward church leadership. And that pull, as Jamieson puts it is not one of a "calling" it is an "aspiring". To fully understand the difference, you'll need to read this book!

From the first chapter, it’s clear that Jamieson is not interested in promoting a modern, platform-driven view of pastoral ministry. Instead, he walks carefully through the biblical and spiritual realities of what it means to be a pastor, or more precisely, an elder. That’s one of the most helpful aspects of the book—its insistence that the title “pastor” is not a personality type, a ministry niche, or a solo calling, but a biblical office, deeply rooted in the character qualifications and shepherding responsibilities outlined in Scripture.

Jamieson writes carefully, and with pastoral wisdom and brotherly care. This is not a lofty or academic treatise, but neither is he simplistic. This book is serious because the calling is serious. And yet, throughout the book, there is a humility in the way Jamieson writes. Jamieson knows the weight of the task and writes like someone who has been shaped by it.

Myself, as someone who is already in ministry and continuing to discern how best to serve the church, this book was more than relevant—it was revealing. I found myself rethinking not just what kind of pastor I might be someday, but what kind of man I am today. The book challenged me to evaluate how I’m leading my family. Am I shepherding my wife with Christlike love? Am I discipling my children intentionally and patiently? These aren’t side questions—they’re central to the path of pastoral ministry, and Jamieson makes that unmistakably clear.

What I appreciated most is that this book is that it's about being the kind of man who is increasingly marked by godliness, humility, sound doctrine, and love for Christ’s people. The pastoral path is not for the ambitious—it’s for the qualified, the tested, the faithful. This book helps you take honest inventory of whether you’re on that path, and what needs to grow before you go any further.

And yet, even with its sobering tone, The Path to Being a Pastor is full of hope. It gives direction without discouragement. It reminds the reader that God, not man, calls pastors—and He uses local churches to shape and affirm them. Aspiring pastors don’t need to walk alone, and they shouldn’t. This book reminds us that pastoral formation is a community project.

If you're considering pastoral ministry—even if you’re just beginning to wonder whether God might be leading you there—read this book. Read it slowly. Read it prayerfully. And if you’re already serving, read it anyway. It will help you evaluate your own heart, clarify your calling, and remind you what faithful ministry is all about.

Jamieson’s work is a gift to the church. It’s biblical, balanced, and beautifully pastoral. I’m better for having read it. This was just awesome.

Title: The Eleventh Metal (Mistborn, #0.5)

The Eleventh Metal by Brandon Sanderson
